reviews.text: The doctor had no record of appointment -- call the doctor directly don't rely on Zocdoc. The doctor that Zocdoc supposedly made an apopintment for me and confirmed through numerous messages di not have a record or know of the appointment.  It wasted 1 1/2 hours of my time. Zocdoc is terrible and I’m sure the…. Clinicians running small practices - beware of this company! They charge providers ridiculous amounts of money to be listed on their platform so the only people on Zocdoc aren’t necessarily good providers - but people who can afford to pay to be on their platform. I’m seeing a lot of zocdoc commercials on tv lately and it’s a bummer because my experience with them as a provider was so disappointing and felt so exploitative. If you run a small healthcare practice DO NOT use this company. They will charge you even when clients no-show or cancel without any flexibility or compromise - I believe their rate is around $70 per “referral.” As a provider I have found this company to be very unsupportive and inflexible

Zocdoc? Unethical, prolly Illegal.. Zocdoc covers for unscrupulous providers, and they abound. I’ve had several providers with advanced credentials and many availabilities call me and before I can say anything, they say there are no such providers available. They offer providers with lesser credentials instead. Zocdoc responded to my ensuing complaint with flowery, sympathetic messages, saying 1).They would investigate (but never tell me the outcome or related consequences); and 2) They would connect me with a list of providers, if I gave some info. THE PROBLEM WAS, THOSE MESSAGES RAN IN LOOPS THAT DID NOT END. In other words, they were talking ZERO ACTION—and had no plans to. I think the strategy is to tire you out, or infuriate you to the point of saying something out of bounds (which I’d never do), so they could rule out service. It’s unethical. And I’m guessing, it’s illegal

DOCTORS BEWARE. DOCTORS BEWARE: Do not use ZocDoc! They effectively steal from your small business and refuse to have your back, no matter how much someone misuses their platform to incur charges on your account. It is very possible to have hundreds of dollars MONTHLY worth of bookings made by patients who either 1. Never respond or show up, and/or 2. Misrepresent their insurance, and ZOCDOC WILL NOT CREDIT YOUR ACCOUNT. In their own words: "The booking fee applies regardless of the outcome of the appointment. In certain cases, fees may be waived, but this is not guaranteed."
From our experience backed by documents, emails, bank statements, and more: this is not true, and there is no case where anything is waived. You may very well find yourself paying upwards of $1000 monthly with ZERO patients in your chair from this platform, and HOURS of front office administrative time wasted, along with time you could have gave other patients. 
Would you pay $1000 a month to have zero patients in your chair, your time wasted, and business disrupted? In my opinion, that is what ZocDoc has begun to exclusively (in effect) offer in the past year. 
Their business model, as well as decision making management, are problematic. Our ZocDoc representative was constantly changing, and regardless, none were any help in making ZocDoc a smart choice for our business. 
From my experience as a Doctor and business owner: this company stole thousands of dollars from me (in effect), and I am certain there are many other practitioners who have had this experience and just have not come forward yet. In fact, many already have, check out this Reddit Thread:
Told me a primary care physician was a…. Told me a primary care physician was a dermatologist, got charged 120 dollars to be told I was in the wrong place

A Provider’s Perspective: The Hidden Cost of Your Appointment. If you use Zocdoc, you should know how it impacts your healthcare providers. This company functions as an advertising platform, and their fee structure is significantly impacting the industry.

Behind every click, Zocdoc charges your doctor or nurse an average of $50–$120 per booking. The most concerning part? Zocdoc often refuses to waive these charges even if the patient cancels or never walks through the door. This means your local clinic is losing money before they even see you.

When you think about the rising cost of healthcare, remember that a large portion of that money isn't going to the people treating you—it’s going to platforms like this. Zocdoc, I'm talking directly to you, myself and other providers our next stop is the Better Business Bureau, followed probably by a law suit, and we will not rest until we force you to stop your predatory policies

Bait and Switch for consumers. ZocDoc allows clinicians to put staff under other staff for availability. While this seems like a great idea to give more options to clients, it is not executed well. Clients are not made aware enough that they are not seeing the "profiled" therapist. Also, when a client leaves a review, if the review was intended for the resource staff, it is actually placed in the "profiled" therapists account. This is for good or bad service. This feels misleading for potential new clients because they will not know if the comments are for the profiled therapist or a different one

I booked a dentist appointment through…. I booked a dentist appointment through ZocDoc, received a confirmation from them but when I arrived at the Dentist they had no record of the appointment. Calling ZocDoc was unsatisfying and took about 1/2 hour for them to claim it was the Dentist offices fault and their call to the office resulted in them actualky finding our booking. I'm still in the dentist office who graciously agreed to fit us in but it looks like a 2 hour wait! 😳

The moral of the story is if you plan on using ZocDoc to book always follow up with the office. You may want to ask yourself though "if I have to call the office anyway why didn't I just book directly in the 1st place?" 🙁

STAY AWAY!!. STAY AWAY! VERY UNPROFESSIONAL!

I had a very frustrating experience with Zocdoc and would not recommend using their platform. The information listed is often inaccurate or misleading, which can easily waste your time when trying to find a provider.

 In my case, the insurance details were not clearly broken down, and what was presented didn’t match the reality once I followed up.
A service like this should make healthcare navigation easier, but instead it created more confusion and inconvenience. If you’re relying on it for accurate insurance compatibility or provider details, be cautious.

Overall, I found it unreliable and not worth the time. I would strongly suggest looking elsewhere unless you’re okay with potentially dealing with incorrect information and unnecessary hassle

How can I put 0 stars. How can I put 0 stars? 
Zocdoc misrepresents itself as a fair platform for patients and providers, but my experience has been the opposite. They allow patients to leave reviews on a specific doctor even if the patient never actually met that doctor. Their policies are vague, only stating that a patient may leave a review if they “visited the practice” — which means reviews can be falsely tied to the wrong provider.

This practice damages a provider’s hard-earned professional reputation, and Zocdoc takes no responsibility for correcting inaccuracies. What they do care about is money: they charge providers around $100 every time a patient books through them (money that comes directly out of the provider’s pocket), and they even impose cancellation fees on providers even if the practice itself never charged the patient a fee.

Zocdoc prioritizes its revenue over fairness, accuracy, and the reputations of the healthcare professionals who use their platform. Providers and patients deserve better.

Update: The patient and I both appealed the review, as it was mistakenly left under the wrong provider. Despite both of our requests for removal or correction, Zocdoc declined to make any changes—even though the patient explicitly asked for it to be edited or removed to reflect the correct provider

Never had this happen in 40 years but after I make a complaint then someone has his ss and is taking out student loans? I wonder who sport med is sharing info with? RUN away from sports med union

ZocDoc willfully conceals illegal and unethical behavior. I am reporting this company to the Better Business Bureau and the State of New York in an effort to get some kind of accountability for their actions, since ZocDoc itself refuses to explain how they can do business this way, legally. Briefly, the issue here is that they have a "closed-loop review system," which means that you can only review a practitioner whom you have scheduled through the ZocDoc platform. 

In my case, I had a psychologist for four months and can easily prove that fact. I am allowed to review him on other platforms because I'm a verified client. But not on ZocDoc. ZocDoc has created its own small world where it shields practitioners from negative reviews by limiting reviewers to only those who scheduled the practitioner through its platform. (Some have called this "a protection racket" and that description fits.)

I am currently awaiting adjudication of a claim filed with the State of Kansas against this particular psychologist. The claim is for an illegal and vindictive patient abandonment, which the psychologist did after I left his services and engaged the services of another therapist on the same telehealth platform.

 This psychologist had no legal right, whatsoever, to interfere with the telehealth platform or another psychologist's caseload, because he does not own the platform. He's only a contractor. Even if he did own it, there are professional guidelines about one psychologist interfering with another psychologist's caseload. The actual owner of the platform in question reinstated my right to see the second psychologist. 

When I reported this illegal and unethical situation to ZocDoc, I was told by their Trust & Safety team that their closed-loop review system was all powerful, basically. Nobody in that department has the slighest clue what is legal or not and they refuse to ask for guidance. I asked them to escalate the case to the legal department. That was refused. I then wrote the legal department an email myself and was completely ignored. 

In summation, ZocDoc chooses to willfully conceal an illegal (and unethical) act on the part of a practitioner on its website. And it refuses to provide any legal justification for doing so. I do not believe that there is any legal justification. That's why I'm going to the State of New York and the Better Business Bureau to get their legal opinion on the matter. At what point do you say "enough?" Does the practitioner have to commit murder of a client, or is willfully damaging the client and preventing the client from speaking to another therapist enough? Where is the line drawn, precisely?
 
I am led to believe that there is no legal or moral justification for ZocDoc's actions, that ZocDoc is just winging it and hoping that nobody challenges their little closed-loop fantasy world.

Between this situation and all the other one-star reviews here, take the hint and do not deal with this company. Who knows what kind of illegal misbehavior they're concealing besides this one instance? Just this one? Dozens? Hundreds? 

In addition to the illegal patient abandonment, the practitioner in question also solicited my participation in Medicare fraud and that fact was likewise ignored by ZocDoc although I would think that to be a glaring red flag for a telehealth platform of any kind.

So again I ask, where does it stop? Where is the dividing line between ZocDoc's protection racket of people breaking the law with its closed-loop review system and ZocDoc telling the truth about who is on its platform? When will ZocDoc be accountable? Or does ZocDoc consider itself to be simply above the law and intends to operate that way until some higher authority (like the state where it's licensed to do business) informs ZocDoc of its legal duties? I guess we're going to find out. It is impossible to talk to these people and hash through differences like adults. Like children, they have to be reported to the principal's office

NO RESULTS FROM EXPENSIVE TREATMENTS. I normally don't leave reviews, but I feel compelled to share my experience with NYDG due to the significant disappointment I've faced with two treatments: Sofwave and Kybella.

After a consultation, I followed the recommendation to undergo Sofwave based on the assurance that it would result in visible improvements. I followed all post-treatment instructions and attended my follow-up appointment. Unfortunately, there was no noticeable change whatsoever--something even the doctor acknowledged when comparing the before-and-after photos taken at the clinic.

Still, I was advised to proceed with Kybella, which was presented as the most effective next step. Despite my initial hesitation due to the underwhelming results from the Sofwave treatment, I went ahead with Kybella in January. Once again, after following all protocols, there were no visible results--something the assistant confirmed during my follow-up appointment in April, after reviewing the updated photographs.

What has been most frustrating is not just the lack of results, but the lack of meaningful response or accountability. I've voiced my concerns directly, yet I've felt dismissed and unheard. As a paying patient, I expected a level of professionalism, transparency, and care. Unfortunately, my experience has not reflected that.

These treatments are expensive and not something most people take lightly. In my case, I feel misled, especially after being assured multiple times that results were guaranteed. I spent $1,600 on the Kybella treatment alone, and I'm incredibly disappointed with how this has been handled.

I hope my experience helps others make informed decisions before committing to any services here. A practice like NYDG should uphold higher standards, and I sincerely hope they reflect on how they manage patient expectations and follow-up care

Primary Care Zocdoc Disaster. As a healthcare provider, I regret ever using this platform. Our practice was charged exorbitant amounts for anyone that gave their name to an appointment on the platform and lost a significant amount of money over time. Unusually high no-show rate and incomplete bookings, for which we were charged nearly $50 per occurrence, resulting in thousands owed in only two months. As a healthcare provider. As a healthcare provider, my experience with Zocdoc has been frustrating and disappointing. While the platform promises to help with patient scheduling and visibility, the reality for providers is far from ideal.

Major Issues:

False Reviews and No Effective Support: One of the most concerning issues with Zocdoc is the inability to get false or misleading reviews removed. Despite providing clear evidence that contradicts a patient's claims, Zocdoc has been unresponsive and unwilling to intervene. This leaves providers vulnerable to damage from malicious or dishonest reviews, which can significantly impact their reputation.

Unresponsive Customer Support: When issues arise, particularly with patient interactions or false reviews, Zocdoc's support is either slow or unhelpful. There is a lack of direct support for providers, and the platform offers no real mechanism to address disputes or concerns effectively. Providers are left to fend for themselves with minimal assistance.

Lack of Transparency and Fairness: The review and moderation process on Zocdoc is opaque and heavily favors patients without consideration for provider context. There is no clear way to dispute unfair reviews, and the process for handling these issues is frustratingly convoluted. As a result, providers are at the mercy of a system that doesn't adequately protect them.

Patient Expectations Are Often Misaligned: Many patients expect immediate responses or overestimate what a provider can do in a limited visit. This can lead to misunderstandings, and Zocdoc offers no support in managing or mitigating these expectations, leaving providers to handle dissatisfied patients on their own.

Conclusion: Zocdoc is a platform that places the burden entirely on providers without offering meaningful support in return. The lack of fair review moderation and poor customer service makes it difficult to recommend this platform to other healthcare professionals. If you're considering joining, be prepared for frustration and minimal assistance when issues arise.

I would not recommend Zocdoc to fellow providers until these issues are addressed. The platform needs a complete overhaul in terms of support, review moderation, and fairness for healthcare professionals

I’ve had a deeply concerning experience…. I’ve had a deeply concerning experience with Zocdoc that raises serious ethical and operational issues for providers.

I have invested thousands of dollars into this platform with the expectation that its systems, billing practices, and policies would be applied fairly and consistently. Unfortunately, that has not been my experience.

One major issue is that Zocdoc appears to penalize providers for circumstances that are entirely outside of their control. For example, I have had multiple instances where patients booked appointments with incorrect insurance information. In one case, a patient selected Cigna during booking, but uploaded a completely different insurance card afterward. Despite this clear discrepancy, I was still charged approximately $600 for the booking, and the charge was not considered disputable.

In another instance, a patient was able to book as out-of-network despite my settings being configured specifically to prevent out-of-network appointments. This was a platform error, not user error. However, I was still charged for that booking, and I received a response stating that the charge was “not disputable.” That is extremely concerning from both a fairness and accountability standpoint.

Additionally, duplicate and erroneous bookings are not adequately addressed. I was charged around $1,000 within minutes due to a patient booking multiple appointments “by accident.” While Zocdoc may internally “flag” these users, there is no meaningful protection or reimbursement for providers who absorb the financial impact.

The broader issue is that providers are expected to pay significant fees per booking, yet there are insufficient safeguards in place to verify insurance accuracy, prevent duplicate bookings, or enforce platform settings. This creates a system where providers assume disproportionate financial risk for errors they did not cause.

Customer support has also been disappointing, particularly around billing disputes, where responses feel rigid and dismissive rather than solution-oriented.

Given the financial stakes involved, platforms like this should have stronger verification processes, clearer accountability measures, and a fair dispute resolution system. Based on my experience, those protections are currently lacking.

I would strongly encourage other providers to carefully evaluate the risks before relying on this platform

Uncaring providers, last minute cancellations. I found it very unprofessional that my trauma therapist, Rebecca Stockland, canceled our appointment less than 30 minutes before the scheduled session yesterday without any explanation. Patients are subject to cancellation fees when appointments are missed or canceled late, yet providers appear able to cancel at the last minute without the same level of accountability.

This was the second time an appointment was canceled, and it has significantly impacted my confidence in the therapeutic relationship.

EMDR and trauma therapy require a great deal of emotional preparation and energy, especially for someone like me with a history of clinical depression, anxiety, and trauma. These cancellations are not simply an inconvenience; yesterday’s cancellation negatively affected my mental health. Not only does it take an extreme amount of vulnerability to find a new therapist, but it also takes significant trust and emotional effort to open up about childhood abuse and other traumatic experiences. Having a session canceled moments before it is scheduled to begin can feel incredibly discouraging and disruptive to the therapeutic process.

I would appreciate confirmation that I will not be charged for the canceled appointment and would like this concern documented as feedback regarding the quality and reliability of care provided

New Outsourced Billing is a Scam. I really enjoyed using Teladoc for years for my therapy appointments and never had a problem, until recently. My insurance has always covered visits fully. My Teladoc account even has the statements for each visit saying I owe zero for each visit. But apparently, in April, they started outsourcing their billing. And their new billing service (my doc bill) is now saying, all of a sudden, I owe full price for my past visits in April and May (even though Teladoc itself says I owe 0). I called both Teladoc and their billing service, and they both said there’s nothing they can do. The billing service person I talked to was rude and unfriendly. They don’t communicate to each other, and it makes no sense that Teladoc would say my visits were covered while the billing service is saying I owe 100 dollars per visit. I will not be using Teladoc again, and I will be fighting these charges

I then spent several days trying to get help and was unable to reach the provider to discuss alternatives while waiting. The request was marked urgent, but I was still left without a solution.

I understand that prior authorization requirements are determined by insurance plans and that providers may not know every plan’s requirements. However, patients should be aware that this can happen. My recommendation is to check your insurance formulary or prescription coverage before ending the appointment, especially if the medication is expensive or brand-name, so you can ask about alternatives if prior authorization may be required.

The actual medical visit was great. The lack of support once the prescription ran into insurance issues is the reason for the 2-star rating

Save Time, Skip MDLive, Go See PCP or Urgent Care. This was one of the most frustrating experiences I've ever had with a doctor. I am writing to provide feedback…. I am writing to provide feedback regarding a recent telehealth encounter that I believe reflects a significant gap in clinical judgment and patient care.

I sought a telehealth consultation after developing acute throat pain that began on Thursday and progressively worsened. The pain initially localized to the right side of my upper throat and quickly became diffuse and severe, making swallowing extremely difficult. By Saturday, I was experiencing significant pain requiring frequent doses of ibuprofen, along with a low-grade fever. I also reported a known exposure to a confirmed case of streptococcal pharyngitis.

Despite clearly communicating these symptoms and risk factors, I was advised that adults have a near zero likelihood of contracting strep throat due to immunity, and that treatment was not urgent. I was told I would need confirmatory testing before any antibiotics could be prescribed.

Following this encounter, my symptoms continued to worsen. I attempted to obtain care through my primary wellness center without success and ultimately sought treatment at an urgent care facility. A rapid strep test confirmed the diagnosis, and I was prescribed antibiotics. Within hours of starting treatment, my symptoms improved dramatically, and by later that same day, my throat pain was largely resolved.

This outcome highlights a missed opportunity for earlier intervention that could have significantly reduced my pain and functional impairment. While I understand the importance of antimicrobial stewardship, the clinical picture I presented—acute onset, severe odynophagia, fever, and known exposure—should have warranted stronger consideration for empiric treatment or, at minimum, more nuanced guidance.

Additionally, I later became aware of a similar case within my household, where another telehealth provider prescribed treatment promptly based on comparable symptoms and exposure history. This inconsistency raises concerns about variability in clinical decision-making across providers.

My intent in sharing this feedback is not simply to express frustration, but to encourage improvement in telehealth assessment protocols. Patients rely on these services for timely care, especially when symptoms are severe and access to in-person evaluation is limited. Greater attention to clinical context, risk factors, and patient-reported severity could help prevent unnecessary suffering and delays in treatment.

I hope this experience can be used as a training opportunity to improve diagnostic reasoning and patient-centered care in future encounters

I scheduled an urgent MDLIVE video…. I scheduled an urgent MDLIVE video visit with Dr. Aquilla Bashir Moosani, MD for my 4-year-old son, who was having persistent barky coughing despite asthma treatment and supportive care at home. I paid $40 expecting a professional pediatric evaluation. What I received was neither safe nor appropriate.

Dr. Moosani never asked to visually assess my child — not once did she ask to see him breathe, hear his cough, or even verify his color or distress level. This was a video visit for a respiratory complaint in a preschool-aged child, and no visual exam was done. Instead, she spent barely two minutes on the call and suggested I “double up on his Breyna inhaler.”

As any parent of an asthmatic or croup-prone child knows, Breyna (Symbicort) already contains a steroid and long-acting bronchodilator. Doubling the dose without assessment can irritate the throat and cause side effects — especially in a 4-year-old. When I questioned this, she offered no explanation, and the visit abruptly ended.

This was not a safe or thorough evaluation. No questions about prior medications, no review of his recent steroid course, and no acknowledgment of my detailed care notes that were uploaded before the visit.

As a mother who has worked closely with multiple pediatricians, neurologists, and specialists for my son, I was shocked by the lack of attention, empathy, and professional diligence. I would strongly caution parents against relying on this provider for pediatric respiratory issues.

MDLIVE should ensure that their pediatric doctors perform actual assessments before giving dosing advice — especially when it involves inhaled steroids. I am requesting a full refund and a review of this encounter for quality and safety compliance.

Parents — always trust your instincts. If a provider won’t look at your child, they haven’t truly evaluated them

10/10 DO NOT RECOMMEND. First, let me start by saying their policy for rescheduling or cancelling your appointment has to be done prior to 24 hours before your scheduled appointment time or you are charged $50. You will also be charged if you "miss" an appointment even if it's a technical issue on the their end. 

 I had a therapy appointment scheduled for this morning. I logged into the portal ten minutes before my appointment time. At five minutes till there will be a blue "join the call" button. This appeared and then I wasn't able to click to join. I refreshed the page, exited the browser, tried again, even tried from my phone, and nothing worked. I called the contact number because by this time it was into my appointment time and I couldn't access it. I didn't want to be charged for something that was clearly a technical issue on their end. I was told that since I had opened a ticket for the issue I would not be charged. THIRTY minutes after my appointment time was supposed to start I got an email from by bank about the charge being attempted. I immediately called them back and they "couldn't see where the charge had been attempted on their end". They also don't have a way to contact the provider to let them know what was going on and that this wasn't just me not showing up to an appointment. Customer service wasn't any help. They told me they would open another ticket, make it a priority, but that they really couldn't tell me if I was going to continue to be charged or not. I just feel like if the issue on their end I shouldn't be charged. Also, I didn't just not show up or cancel my appointment, I was unable to login through their website because of an issue on their end. How is that my fault? I also have a screenshots and videos of the issue so that could see what was actually happening. My last call ended with the rep telling me she would send me an email so I could send the the documentation to see if it would be acceptable proof of the incident. It's been 3 hours since that call and I still haven't received an email. I get that technical issues happen. I'm not upset at that. I am upset that they don't care that it was their error and are making me pay for something I had zero control over. It's bad enough that if someone had a true emergency and had to reschedule or cancel within 24 hours of their appointment that they would be charged as well, but when the issue is on their side and they are punishing the patient for that - that's wrong. I have had other issues with them, trying to schedule with providers and the providers cancel the appointments, or send a request to a provider for availability and no one responds.I even had a provider be rude and tell me "I was wasting her time" because I didn't know where I needed to start with therapy, just that I wanted to start it.  So this is actually just the final straw for me, and I will no longer use them. It's provided through BCBS and yes, it's an easy way to make therapy appointments but I'll find somewhere else to go. If this is the way they treat their patients then I want nothing to do with them, and they will not be getting my business. 10/10 do not recommend

1 Star Is Too Generous — A Total Healthcare Failure. I used MDLIVE for a 3 AM virtual visit because I was in pain and needed antibiotics urgently. I was told the prescription would be sent immediately to a 24-hour pharmacy so I wouldn’t have to wait for care.

Instead, I waited over 7 hours just to get the prescription sent — and when it finally was, the medication was prescribed in a form that my insurance didn’t cover. The pharmacy explained that capsules (which cost around $10) were covered, but MDLIVE had written it for tablets (which cost over $70). The pharmacist couldn’t substitute it — only the prescribing doctor could change it.

I called my insurance (Cigna), and they confirmed that only the provider can change the prescription — but MDLIVE’s doctor was completely unreachable. No call back. No email. No follow-up. Nothing.

At the time I’m writing this review, it’s been over 14 hours since my appointment, and I still don’t have a filled prescription.

This entire experience has been an exhausting waste of time and money. The provider was unprofessional, barely visible on screen, didn’t let me explain my symptoms, and the “After Care Instructions” just linked to a generic blog about insurance — not medical guidance.

MDLIVE failed on every level:
	•	Failed to deliver timely care
	•	Failed to ensure insurance compatibility
	•	Failed to provide a reachable provider for follow-up
	•	Failed to protect patient safety

I’ve requested a refund and was told it would take up to 45 business days. Frankly, I don’t think this company should be allowed to call itself a healthcare provider. I wouldn’t recommend MDLIVE to anyone — even in an emergency. Go literally anywhere else

Do not recommend. I have been using MDLive (for Cigna) for about 5 years. In short, it was not too bad at first, but as time has been progressing, the quality of its service continues to decline. I had my PCP and behavioral health teams on MDLive. Appointment management is terrible: one needs to cancel an appointment before being able to reschedule one, unless your provider schedules your follow-ups with you, it can take months to get another appointment. Lab work has been "experiencing a national outage" for about 3 years now, so lab orders take at least one week to get through, if they even make it to the lab. Referrals do not work properly. MDLive pushes Express Scripts pharmacy delivery services hard, but this service is so bad (delayed prescriptions, issues with insurance, can't get a hold of my MDLive PCP, medication doesn't arrive on time), I opted out of it. Save yourself the stress and frustration and see an in-person provider for recurring appointments. Urgent Care and one-off appointments work fine, solely cause alternatives aren't usually better, but do NOT recommend recurring care through MDLive

Hoo boy! After today’s frustrating experience.... Hoo boy! After today’s frustrating experience with MDLive, I reviewed online feedback and found many others reported similar issues. My encounter involved rude, abrupt, and dismissive physician assistants (PAs) who didn’t listen and hung up before resolving my concerns, reflecting a common experience.

During my initial call, the first PA hung up before completing our conversation. After waiting 15 minutes without a callback, I called again and reached a different PA. This second PA stated they couldn’t prescribe the medication the first PA had indicated was possible, citing MDLive’s “policy,” which raised my suspicions about inconsistent practices. She offered to connect me with the original PA but then hung up. After 30 minutes with no follow-up, I called the support number to speak with the first PA. I struggled to understand him due to a heavy accent, and he too hung up. Both PAs seemed rushed, frequently interrupting me, suggesting they were constrained by a call time limit. I have filed a complaint with Cigna and plan to submit a grievance to Medicare. Several reviews highlight similar issues with this Cigna-affiliated service, and I’m astonished Cigna continues to partner with MDLive given these consistent problems. This will be my last encounter with them

I would never use MDLife again but they did give me a refund.. After tracking my blood pressure for awhile and saw how high it was getting (175/103) I decided to book an appt with my PCP but she is booked for several weeks so I thought I should try to get a temporary prescription until I could see my doc. Since my daughter recently got a BP script from Teladoc I knew it was possible to get that kind of script from a telehealth visit. So, when I saw that MDLive is in my insurance network I thought I could get a temporary script from them until I see my doc. I filled out the pre-visit form in which I detailed my BP issue so the doctor would be apprised beforehand but when he called he asked me what I needed help with, so I recounted everything I had already written.  He proceeded to tell me that it was "outside his scope" to write me even a temporary script "because he isn't my PC doc"...WTH?? Does anyone have a primary care doctor at MDLive? Why do they even exist if that's the case?   He told me the usual BP advice that I need to cut down on salt, caffeine, etc...as if that was any sort of real help that someone would be willing to pay for. He then went on to say that if my BP got any higher that I should go to urgent care!!

Afterward, I called Teladoc and two other telehealth services to ask if prescribing BP meds is within their scope of things they prescribe and the answer was "yes" all 3 times!  

I have called customer service twice now. The first CS agent confirmed that the doctors ARE allowed to call in scripts for BP meds since they are not narcotics or restricted. Both times I have called customer service I have been told that my case is being "escalated" to a supervisor and someone would call me back soon...but so far NO CALLS! 

So, just know going in, since they aren't your primary care doctor, there's a good chance that they will take your money and DO ABSOLUTELY NOTHING ELSE!

9-8-2025: So I upgraded from a 1-star to a 3-star because a supervisor finally called me back and she was super professional and nice, gave me a refund and explained that the reason the doctor couldn't give me a prescription was because the virtual appt option I chose was for an "urgent care" appt and only a virtual call to a primary care doctor could have resulted in a script. That makes absolutely no sense to me and when I was scheduling the virtual visit it didn't ask which kind of appt I wanted. Also, I explained to her that another problem I have with this situation and her explanation is that I had to detail what my issue was IN ORDER TO SET UP THE APPT so, as I told her, he would have read and already known what I needed help with and shouldn't have chosen to make the call if he'd known that he wasn't going to be able to do anything to help me. They need to have it built into their system to reject appt's if the pre-appt information deems the appt incompatible with their protocols of what they won't treat

To say I'm dissatisfied is an understatement. To say I'm dissatisfied with the time it took to resolve a simple billing issue is an understatement. 

My original email to Amwell support about the issue was on April 14, 2024. I was then informed that it could take 4–6 weeks for investigation. After 6 weeks of silence, I sent a follow-up email in the support thread on June 4, 2024 asking for an update. No response. I then called Amwell customer support, and they said they would escalate the ticket. Still no response after several more weeks. I sent yet another email in the support thread on June 24, 2024. Still nothing. I called Amwell customer support again, and they said they simply weren't sure why it was taking so long, but that they would try to escalate it again. Beware, not convenient or easy. I paid for this service to go along with my short term medical coverage. I would be put in queue, kicked out, transferred to a new doctor all of the sudden, told I was next, told my appointment had started, etc and then be kicked back out to the queue again. It was absolutely ridiculous. 

So here I am again in July, trying to see a doctor, and it's been an hour. Website said I was next, I waited almost 25 minutes and was kicked out. I called and was set up a phone call appointment but at the same time was able to get back into the queue for the doctor who had "no wait time". 

It's been another 30 minutes and I have not received a phone call nor has the doctor with no wait come online but I did get a text saying they were ready to see me almost 15 minutes ago and to return to my computer (which I had never left). Nada. Nothing. No change. Still just waiting. Their system is completely screwed up and it makes no sense to do this to paying customers.

It would be easier to go to the Urgent Care and pay $75 but they're not open right now. I will never pay for this service again and I suggest you don't either.

ETA: I did end up getting a call from a doctor and spent about 1 hour and 15 minutes total making calls and scheduling appointments and thinking I was "next in line" which was far less than last time

They'll just lie to you and have no mechanisms to amend it. This was a lie. You have to manually call in and tell them to submit the claim. You could submit it yourself... if your company accepts patient-submitted claims, which mine does not. The timeline then is 45-60 days *just to submit the claim* (you have the documentation, how exactly does it take 45-60 days to transmit it?). Then it is 4-6 weeks for the company to process it. Then there is no timeline whatsoever given for Amwell to process reimbursement. Meanwhile this $300 charge for a 3-minute Rx renewal is sitting on a credit card accumulating interest, and there is no way to escalate the situation. Super ethical business

I told them the pharmacy was closed and they said it was no problem -- that they would leave a message with the proper prescription information on the pharmacy voicemail. I don’t want to drop her name on a public platform because I don’t want my review removed.

For anyone out there working a solid program and are on the road to recovery- don’t book an appointment with Amwell. I’m going back to Betterhelp because it truly was better

Not happy - At all!. In the past, I have had good experiences with amwell and have even recommended them. I need to see a dr. Maybe every couple of years for a strep infection. I know how my body reacts to strep, how my throat feels, and how it looks. Before, the doctor has prescribed antibiotics and I’ve gotten better. Unfortunately, this time I also happened to have what I think is strep plus the dreaded omnicron. I called 2 nights in a row with throat pain that was unbearable. Both doctors told me - it’s just another symptom of Covid. You’ll be fine in a few days. One prescribed a drug for pain which had me throwing up all night. Imagine an aleady sore throat and dry heaving all night long! The next doctor said - oh that was probably the meds. Discontinue those. Well, duh! I already threw them away (they cost $40 btw). What is the harm in prescribing an antibiotic for a patient?! Especially  one that calls once every 2 or 3 years? Why won’t they just help? With Covid, my only other option is the ER. That’ll be a $3k visit for them to tell me I have strep. All of my Covid symptoms are gone, but the sore throat is unbearable. Ridiculous that these cocky know it all doctors think they can tell me that I don’t know anything from personal experiences. Pisses me off. I wish I could go on the app and rate these 2 men, personally! 🤬

Charged for services NOT rendered, no refund promised, just a month wait to find out. I was unable to find a therapist appointment in person due to extremely long waits for a first appointment. Amwell was recommended to me as a quick and effective alternative. Not that it is relevant here, but I have worked in a COVID ICU for a year and a half now and finally felt ready to talk to someone about my anxiety. 

I signed up for AMWELL and submitted my insurance information, and made an appointment with a therapist. I signed into my virtual appointment and waited for 45 minutes, then received a message on the app that the doctor could not keep the appointment and canceled. The only way to exit out of that screen was to click the "cancel" button, so (my mistake I guess) that is what I did. There was no other button. Not even a text box to reply to the message that specifically asked me if I had any questions. I am unsure how it makes sense to ask me if I have questions but provide no way for me to respond with one.

I read beforehand that the charge for canceling an appointment was $40. This should not be relevant because it was the doctor who canceled, not me, but I bring that up because I was charged $109 (not $40). 

I then called the number the app provided and told them what happened. The customer service rep submitted a ticket, whatever that means, and told me I would hear back within one month. A month!?

So basically, I made an appointment to speak to someone about my severe anxiety, got charged full price for it despite the doctor canceling on me, was not promised I would even get refunded, and told I may have to wait an entire month before I even find out if I will get my money back. This app just added to the problem it was designed to help with. It is not well designed at all, clearly, because my only way to exit the app was to click the "cancel" button (after myself having been canceled on) and subsequently get charged for services NOT rendered. And again, was not even promised a refund, just a ridiculously long wait. Do not recommend

I had a very disappointing and…. I had a very disappointing and frustrating experience during my recent telehealth appointment with this provider. I scheduled the visit because I am experiencing significant work-related stress, severe sleep disruption, exhaustion, and emotional strain that has begun affecting my ability to function and work.

During the session I explained in detail that the situation at my job has escalated to the point where I have been missing work and feeling overwhelmed. Instead of acknowledging the seriousness of what I was describing, the provider repeatedly reframed workplace disciplinary actions as “positive feedback” and suggested I should simply use them to “do better next time,” rather than addressing the impact the situation was having on my mental health.

At several points I explained that I was extremely stressed, agitated, and at my breaking point with the situation. I also requested documentation confirming that I had attended the appointment, as I have been struggling this week and had missed work. The provider refused to provide even a basic doctor’s note or visit confirmation.

What made the interaction particularly upsetting was that when I expressed my concern about not receiving any documentation or support, the provider appeared to smirk during portions of the conversation. That reaction felt dismissive and unprofessional, especially given that I was explaining serious stress and emotional distress.

When I stated that I felt the interaction was being handled poorly and that I intended to report the experience, the provider abruptly ended the call.

Patients seeking mental health care should feel heard, respected, and taken seriously. Unfortunately, this interaction left me feeling dismissed and unsupported at a time when I was actively seeking help for significant stress and emotional strain.

I hope the practice reviews how patients in vulnerable situations are treated during telehealth appointments so that others do not leave feeling the same way

Their AI and Operating System is Grossly Dysfunctional. The AI that is heavily used by Doctor on Demand is dysfunctional. We had a very disappointing experience…. We had a very disappointing experience using Doctor on Demand for my minor daughter. On multiple occasions, her therapist failed to attend scheduled appointments with no notice, no call, and no follow-up. These repeated no-call/no-shows were disruptive and unacceptable, especially for a child receiving mental health care.

During our most recent appointment, I experienced technical issues accessing the platform and contacted Doctor on Demand immediately to report the problem. Despite notifying support in real time, I was still charged $129 for the appointment. Resolving this charge became another ordeal, involving nonfunctional payment links and repeated attempts to get help by phone and message with little meaningful support.

I was also alarmed by guidance provided early in our care. Due to my divorce, staff advised me to share my login credentials with my ex-husband so he could access our daughter’s care. This directly contradicts basic HIPAA privacy standards and placed my personal protected health information at risk.

Overall, our experience reflected poor communication, improper billing practices, and inadequate attention to patient privacy. As a result, we have discontinued using Doctor on Demand. I would not recommend this service, particularly for families seeking reliable and professional care for minors

Health tap is a tentacle. Health tap is a tentacle of the healthcare and health insurance industrial complex. At some point - perhaps a few decades ago -medical professionals in all areas of healthcare decided to throw out the Hippocratic Oath and replace it with their lust for higher incomes declaring conditions to be worse than they really are, which, of course, means more treatment and more income. I tried Health Tap just to see if it confirmed the conclusions I’ve stated above - conclusions I’ve come to from my own horrific experiences with doctors and hospitals over several decades. What the one star and two star reviews are saying is true. The advice that is given by the doctors is cursory and isn’t something that one can’t find on their own by doing an internet search on symptoms. and I’m not surprised that they are being over billed, and/or double billed, etc. etc. Also, the price that customers are paying for this cursory advice is atrocious. Those who know the reality of our “ modern“ healthcare, and health insurance system shouldn’t be surprised at the lack of ethics that is ubiquitous in these institutions or that they would move online as an easier way to make good money, without really doing much, if any, for many people, and make a bundle doing it. 

Sure enough, after explaining what I will call a “female problem” to one of Health Tap’s doctors I was simply told that I should go see a doctor in person. It wasn’t a condition that I needed to see a doctor about. So I realized that this is one trick that is used by some doctors in the network, believing that everyone that calls in for advice is ignorant, So without much concern to what subscribers are referring to why not simply tell them to do that (go see a real doctor in person). On the surface, perfectly legal, but in reality, unethical, because the telehealth visit was extremely short and Health Tap got their fee upfront. So after the consultation I immediately canceled my membership. If one looks very carefully at the wording of the various reviews on Trust Pilot, they will see that the one and two star reviews are very detailed in relating their experiences, and they seem to have for the most part, a common theme and an angry tone. The five star reviews are all bright and cheery. And, frankly, I believe very few of those glowing reviews aren’t fake. Another thing to take notice of is Health Tap’s responses to the negative reviews. With one or two exceptions they are all pretty much the same: “We are very sorry that you had such a negative experience. Please contact us” and so on and so forth. Those responses from Healthtap in my opinion are just as fake as most of the positive reviews. The movement by the healthcare industry to jump on the telehealth bandwagon is ginormous. Health tap could be more honestly named WEALTH TAP

Branding HealthTap. Your organization requests data that 'providers' willingly volunteer. Feed AI. Create better algorithms? Tapping into physician mindset understand that it's not a cash and carry trip, rather an understanding (through corporate), that docs intuitively respond to curious scenarios—often perplexing and heightening the challenge—A chess game of sorts where extraction is not only compelling, it's delightful. Brilliant. But at some point there's a juncture: How much time can I put into this? At least some fair equitable compensation for time—And, after all, that commodity cannot be replaced. Get a trophy get a prize get a pat on the back and blah blah blah. "You're number one or two," and wisely constructed a system of delivering healthcare information at a fee. Hey. I've been doing this a long—four decades—and sure, I'll be inclined to help where I can when I can. But you folks charge patients a fee—At this point a jolt of 'what am I doing this for?" pops in. You are using docs/providers/prescribers without considerations. Clearly YOUR time is worth something, but not the good natured, dedicated people on the front line. I don't know. You're a business functioning to remain solvent, perhaps a low hanging fruit to be picked up by VC. But my view is you are using willing 'providers' at no cost. There are better ways to retain participants to make this company function optimally with clarity, and due participation in the machinations. The business model is simple enough to understand: Offer good hearted 'providers' to provide data which you can parlay into a databank usable by —Including but not limited to: Insurance companies, pharma, patients with 'doorknob' questions, and, by consensus gather enough material to market as a 'go to' site that if you've got a health related issue, just tap in about health—HealthTap. Cute name, but after a span of time HT is not a household ideation, rather a second, second thought. But a few bucks here and there from staging patients with issues…No no no, that's not where the serious capital comes from. Gather enough data and you can sell the whole kit and caboodle - Create an AP, take it public (excuse me, I'm getting ahead of myself). You have remarkably talented physicians of all specialties who pour over the material and -advantageously tickle them with a carrot that's not a carrot it's an internal motivator to help people. Whoever the MD MBA advisor had an idea, you've got a databank to market…Sure, you get folks vying to provide information, maybe to market their own practices, which I doubt is central to board certified minds to feed the monkey (if you will the liberty of language). Packaged and shopped: Biggest databank on the planet. Got it. By taking the elemental ideals of good hearted people give them a nod and computer generated wink. Frankly, it has too many holes (as a marketable construct) to stand up over time. Still not branded. Still not part of daily life (globally) - Okay, I know folks who've given in and volunteered…However there are better ways to attract VC/branding/and universal acceptance as the "Go To" location on the web

Psychological Treatment Changes Needed. The program is great from the view of a medical patient.  However, from the viewpoint of patients with psychological conditions, the program is lacking. 

First, the rates for brief consultations may be adequate, but there is rarely such a thing as “brief” therapy in the way medical consultations occur.  “Brief” in psychotherapy still requires a personal, family  and medication history.  

Secondly, one or two follow up sessions are often necessary.  As follow-up sessions are determined, the 30-50 minute sessions are an additional fee that confuse many patients who barely comprehended the medical service they signed onto.  

SOAP notes are convenient for medical cases, but psychological notes often entail two files. The separate file is for the purpose of noting a process that must build toward obtaining information patients conceal from doctors until a level of trust is established over several sessions.  For example, if a patient complains about depression, the art of the therapist is to build toward revealing any hidden causes, such as abuse or substance dependence.  

Finally, psychological records are generally only peer reviewed by other trained therapists. In addition, the separate case notes can not be subpoenaed.  When a patient knows that any doctor can access their secrets, they are unlikely to be willing to reveal them.  

To make this program viable for psychological treatment, these issues need to be addressed!

PlushCare: CAUTION!. Dr. Alexander Drew, who somehow has 4.5 stars on the app yet there is no way to rate people on the app or anywhere that I can find, had an appointment with me while I was working and I just decided to cash pay (around $160) because I did not have my insurance card & couldn’t leave work but was sick. I had a self-diagnosed UTI confirmed by a test I took from Walgreens, symptoms, and experience. I also had a sinus infection. 
Also self diagnosed, but I usually get maybe one per year and after about a month and symptoms were getting worse and persisting, and having had several sinus infections in the past, I knew what it was. I asked him for antibiotics that would cover both the uti and the sinus infection and knew ahead of time it was Levaquin that was the appropriate choice. I also mentioned that I typically take urogesic blue but was out and believe if I had it, I wouldn’t have the uti. With urogesic, I only needed to take it five or six times a year maybe when I felt like I had engaged in any activity or not drank enough water, swam in dirty water, or done something that would lead to a UTI. After getting urogesic, I went from 5 to 6 UTIs a year down to one every few years. I’m a pharmacist so I know that urogesic blue can help with prevention. I ran out of it and wasn’t able to take the urogesic blue and developed a UTI. I also know he prescribed the antibiotic twice on accident to two different pharmacies. This is a strong antibiotic with a black box warning regarding severe, disabling, and potentially permanent side effects, including tendon rupture, tendinitis, peripheral neuropathy, and central nervous system effects. Good thing I’m a pharmacist and knew not to take both. After a couple of days I messaged PlushCare about the urogesic blue thinking he possibly meant to call in urogesic and Levaquin, still giving him the benefit of the doubt, he said I would have to book another appointment because we never spoke about that. That is 100% not true. I requested the video or transcript of the call. They wouldn’t provide it, but did confirm that at least a transcript existed. The company refused to read the transcripts that they keep of these appointments where I do mention it. 

This company is just like every other company that is THE problem in healthcare right now, in my opinion. Fake reviews? I asked to be directed to the reviews of the physician so I could write one and not only could I not figure out how to review him, the company couldn’t tell me either. In my opinion, this is a money-centric patient mill. Book another appointment for what? We had it. Another way to get $. Rather than serve the patient outside of the appointment, and escribe (takes 10 seconds) an extremely minor drug used for uti prevention, he refused to prescribe it and said I would need another appointment. Scam? 

They also automatically and covertly sign you up for a $20 per month subscription. It is very hidden and difficult to cancel. Plushcare is ultimately a scam at worst & misleading at best, in my opinion. And as soon as I figure out how the doctors are “rated” I will make sure that I rate this person. I’m very interested if anyone will ever explain to me where those reviews come from. He is not held accountable for prescribing Levaquin for 28 days which is inappropriate as this is a strong antibiotic with an FDA black box warning and is withholding other medication I need and should have gotten from that appointment that I paid for?

 Also, here is what it looks like when you click the “link” they provide to cancel your membership. (Since I can’t add the photo I will copy and paste the text) 

It says:

Sorry, we couldn't find what you were looking for.
The link you followed may be broken or the page may have been removed. If you would like help, please give us a call at (855) 913-0255. 

So I called. A grievance was filed. And I was told the $20 fee that is very covertly added to be automatically withdrawn from your account monthly, is not automatically added if you call to make the appointment vs using the app. But the whole point is using the app/online because that’s where you go to have your telehealth visit. This is misleading and wrong. This is only the first platform I will be addressing their standards and practices, and holding them accountable. As I said before I’m a pharmacist and I see how broken the healthcare system is and I work with a very vulnerable set of patients who this could easily be confused by Plushcare. As healthcare providers our oath is “first do no harm” and this could harm several vulnerable people. It could also help a lot of people if it was a patient care first, money later approach. But it isn’t in my opinion so ask the right questions and know how things work. While this would be a great app for the elderly, or the homebound, I would never trust this company to serve my own grandmother

They told me I was ineligible, promised a refund, and then charged me for 7 months.. In August 2025, a PlushCare representative called me to inform me I was ineligible for the service and medication I had requested. She assured me I would be refunded. I immediately logged into my account, cancelled my membership, and removed my payment method entirely.
I never received a refund. I never received a single email, invoice, or any communication from PlushCare again. I assumed the matter was closed.
Seven months later, I discovered they had been charging my credit card $19.99 every single month — plus an additional $99 charge the same day they told me I was ineligible. Nearly $260 in unauthorized charges on an account I was told was closed, for a service I was told I couldn't even use.
This isn't a billing error. This is a company that continued charging a cancelled account, with a removed payment method, with zero communication, for over half a year. When I tried to dispute the charges with my bank, I discovered PlushCare had been silently updating my card details through Visa's account updater system to keep the charges going.
I have filed a formal demand for refund and will be escalating to the CFPB and FTC if not resolved within 7 days.
Do not give this company your payment information

For example, you should NOT be required to be seen (and charged), and your insurance company should not have to cover visits, every 3 months for medication refills - especially refills of a common medication for which frequent medical monitoring is rarely (if ever) recommended. mislading borderline fraud. I visited PlushCare’s website to schedule an appointment regarding weight-loss medication. The site stated that the consultation would be a $30 copay with insurance, plus a free trial membership ($19.99/month afterward). Based on this information, I proceeded to schedule a same-day appointment.

The appointment lasted no more than 10 minutes, and I was informed that I would need bloodwork and a follow-up appointment before possibly receiving a prescription. At that point, I decided it would be more practical to follow up in person with my primary care provider.

Immediately after the appointment, I was charged $105 twice (total $210) instead of the advertised $30 copay. When I contacted customer service, I was told that the appointment cost $250 and that I needed to submit it through insurance — information that was not clearly disclosed at any point while signing up, scheduling, or meeting with the provider. I also saw that patients without insurance pay approximately $129, so being charged more than that while using insurance is extremely concerning.

This experience felt misleading and non-transparent, and I would not have scheduled the appointment had accurate pricing been clearly presented

Extremely Unreliable and Disrespectful to Patients’ Time. I scheduled three appointments in one day through PlushCare, and every single one failed because the doctors either no-showed or canceled last minute.

The first doctor never showed up, even after I waited for 20 minutes.

I rescheduled for later that day — the second doctor also didn’t show up.

I tried a third time, and that doctor canceled right before the appointment without any explanation or message.

By the end of the day, I had wasted hours waiting and rescheduling, paid $120 for nothing, and received no medical care at all.

What makes this worse is the one-sided responsibility: patients get constant reminders to show up and be on time, but the doctors apparently can no-show with no accountability or communication. It’s incredibly frustrating and humiliating to have your time and health treated like it doesn’t matter.

PlushCare may have an appealing idea — convenient virtual healthcare — but the execution is terrible. If the doctors don’t actually show up for scheduled appointments, the whole system fails. Until they fix these reliability and communication issues, I would strongly discourage anyone from subscribing or using this service

Sleazy renewal model.. The service is relatively convenient.  Doctors are available within 6 hours usually, and they are ontime.  If they can't help you and send you to a doctor, they don't charge you.  However, the auto-renewal business model is a little sleazy.  If you subscribe, you are automatically signed up to auto renew.  You can't get a refund if they renew you automatically.  You can cancel (and you get a warning that you will lose care even if they just charged you for a year), but you will be RESUBSCRIBED to AUTORENEW if you book an appointment in the year that you paid for it.  So, to prevent auto renewal, you have to cancel the service every time you book an appointment

Buyer Beware. Buyer Beware. Malpractice 

My experience with Hims Weight Loss has been deeply disappointing and raises serious concerns regarding continuity of care.

I enrolled in and paid for a 360-day weight loss program, completed all required intake forms, check-ins, and program requirements, and followed the prescribed treatment plan exactly as directed.

The problem began when Hims failed to ship a scheduled medication refill. As my medication supply ran out, I repeatedly contacted support seeking assistance to maintain continuity of treatment.

What followed was a revolving door of customer service representatives and physicians. Over multiple interactions, I was required to repeatedly explain my situation to new providers who appeared unfamiliar with the history of my case. Despite numerous messages, no single provider or case manager assumed ownership of the issue.

Rather than addressing the immediate problem of maintaining continuity of treatment, discussions shifted toward changing medications, pricing alternatives, and partial refunds. While refunds may address financial concerns, they do not address the interruption of a medical treatment plan already in progress.

My concern is not merely customer service. It is continuity of care, care coordination, and accountability.

I encourage prospective customers to carefully consider whether a highly distributed telehealth model with multiple rotating providers is consistent with their expectations for ongoing medical management.

This review reflects my personal experience and is supported by documented communications with Hims

I had a very disappointing experience…. I had a very disappointing experience with Hims.

I purchased Hims products hoping to reduce hair loss and improve hair growth. Unfortunately, the opposite happened. After using the product, I experienced increased hair loss and saw a negative impact on my hair compared to before I started treatment.

To make matters worse, Hims continued charging me through its automatic refill program. They shipped a refill that I never opened or used, yet they refused to accept a return or provide any refund.

The customer service experience was equally frustrating. Instead of helping resolve the issue, the support team simply repeated their policy and showed little interest in addressing my concerns.

In my experience, Hims has had both a physical and financial impact on me. If you’re considering hair loss treatment, I would recommend carefully researching your options and considering a local clinic or specialist. While local treatments may cost more upfront, you may receive better support, personalized care, and accountability.

Overall, I am very dissatisfied with both the product and the customer service provided by Hims

Over the last year I've been a regular…. Over the last year I've been a regular customer of Hims for their GLP-1 compounded product.  I never really had any issues until this month. The long and short is that Hims suffers from a logistical problem and likely compliance issues with the FDA, still...

Regarding the logistics, I've had numerous cold chain shipments sent to me without any issues. That changed this month and for absolutely no reason.  What I find curious is that Hims claims they do. It ship via the Postal Service and especially not to Post Office boxes.  Where I live, we do NOT have rural mail delivery. FedEx and UPS deliver to my door, but the postal service does not. 

I was asked to provide another shipping address and I gave them my physical street address as requested before, but now, they say that the GLP-1 would be shipped by the postal service and I needed to provide another address.  That being said, the only other address I have is a PO Box.  

This issue was never resolved and frankly, after reading some of the warning letters issued by the FDA to Hims/Hers, I am not reassured that anything they provided was completely safe or met the minimum requirements of FDA/CDER

Amazon One Medical is trash. Amazon One Medical is trash. Don't waste your time or money. Providers do not care or listen to you. They shove you to an online assessment that takes 20 minutes to complete then they base their ENTIRE treatment plan (or lack of) solely off how you scored on the assessment. They completely dismiss everything you say, all of your lived experiences, and all of your symptoms are completely ignored. All they use for your treatment is the results of an online assessment. 

You can be perfectly healthy and intentionally fail the online assessment and they will prescribe you medication. Or, in my case, have debilitating symptoms that make it impossible to function, score average on the assessment and get denied treatment. 

This place is a joke. The providers are basically relying on AI generated assessments, responses and treatment. They will disregard your actual symptoms and ignore what you are physically and mentally experiencing, instead relying solely on what a 20 minute online tests tells them

Classic billing trap. Beware of surprise "double billing" fees.. I scheduled a routine annual physical—which should be 100% covered by preventive care—but walked out with a surprise $500+ bill. Because the doctor asked about my basic medical history (a chronic condition), One Medical sneakily tacked on an extra "sick visit" office code (99203) without my knowledge or consent. When I disputed this predatory split-billing practice, customer service hid behind standard corporate policy. Infuriating bait-and-switch. I canceled my membership and will not be back

One Medical and Amazon Pharmacy should…. One Medical and Amazon Pharmacy should work hand in glove, no? Nope. The whole system is clunky and non-optimal. I paid for a virtual visit, was given “no refills” for a common, non-addictive medicine and now that I do need a refill I’m told by the Amazon pharmacy that I need to pay for another virtual visit for a refill. What? It’s infuriating and a Byzantine nightmare to navigate,  just like all healthcare in the USA. If the deficits in the system are simply due to incompetence (probably) then it’s enraging that such critical infrastructure as healthcare should be so broken. Or, it’s working as designed: it’s a snare to ensure anyone without financial means sickens and dies. I suspect the corporate overlords 
1. Really don’t care about the survival of the “average American”
2. If the fallout of late stage capitalism’s indifference is loss of life on a mass scale then so much the better because they want to replace humans with ai anyway 
Moral of the story: don’t trust Amazon with something as dear as your health

Very disappointing billing experience…. Very disappointing billing experience with One Medical.

I received an unexpectedly large patient responsibility amount for a telehealth visit and attempted to resolve the matter directly through their billing team. While the responses were polite, they largely consisted of generic explanations about insurance deductibles and reimbursement policies rather than addressing my actual concerns regarding the coding level billed and the lack of upfront transparency around costs.

What frustrated me most was the inability to obtain a substantive explanation or supporting documentation despite multiple requests. The clinical interaction itself was fine, but the billing and dispute-resolution process left me feeling dismissed as a patient.

I would strongly encourage patients to verify coverage, deductible exposure, and potential visit coding implications before scheduling telehealth appointments with One Medical

Amazon One (Out of Five Stars) Medical Was the Worst Online Customer Experience I Have Ever Had. Amazon One Medical yielded my first negative experience with any Amazon product in my life thus far. I strongly recommend that Amazon management spin this one off because it is a potential brand destroyer.

The company is one of several innovators that basically draws a significant stream of income through deceiving its customers with limited attention by appearing to offer huge discounts on a particular service, but actually selling a similar sounding but quite different service, c.f.
Homeaglow. In this case, One Medical looks sells a "membership" that gives you "access" to telemedicine but not "insurance" itself so be aware. 

I signed up for it because it was advertised as a free two week membership offering access to telehealth services, which sounded close enough to insurance. I was hoping to get a cheap referral for a difficult to obtain through my primary health insurance provider. I was ultimately billed $412 for a 12 minute call with a nurse practitioner* who could make no prescriptions, but only make outpatient referrals - none of which were covered by primary health insurance provider. The customer service phone line was actually deactivated when I called it. Via calling Amazon's overarching health business helpline, I was able to get in touch a generally unsympathetic representative from Amazon One Medical. Essentially, you are upcharged if you report that you have insurance (??) and, if you call to complain, Amazon One Medical offers to charge you only the rate you would have if you were uninsured.

As a health-conscious customer, I would stay as far away from this product as possible if you are in need of discount medical services. If you must buy it, read all the fine print, and assume that you are dealing with an extremely cynical operator on the other side. I am not the type of person to negatively review products and try to be forgiving in general, but my overall customer experience here was so unpleasant that I felt compelled to leave this review in the public interest.

*The nurse practitioner I was referred to was perfectly fine given the constraints of the platform, which perhaps keeps this review at 1/5 rather than 0/5 or < 0/5

Serious Concerns About Billing and Medical Record Accuracy. People read these like stories, so give them just a bit more:

I’ve been a member of One Medical for some time, and the difference in quality over the past year is hard to ignore.

What prompted this review was a billing issue that should have been straightforward. I was charged for injections that were never administered. I flagged it, expecting a simple correction. Instead, I was told their records are correct and that I’m responsible for the charges.

That response is what’s most concerning. If a provider can’t reconcile what actually happened during a visit with what’s documented, that’s not just frustrating, it raises real concerns about the reliability of their medical records.

This used to feel like a modern, patient-first experience. Now it feels operationally inconsistent and difficult to trust.

I’d encourage anyone considering joining to look closely at recent reviews and weigh whether this is the level of care and accuracy you’re comfortable with

Women's Health. I had a follow-up appointment at One Medical regarding perimenopause care after previously seeing Dr. Shin Jung Yoon. My appointment was scheduled for 1:00pm; I arrived at 1:01 and was brought back at 1:07.

During the visit, I clearly explained the two main reasons I was there and where I hoped to focus. However, much of the appointment was spent revisiting prior lab work, which I was told was not valid due to timing and fasting issues.

When asked about the prescribed medication, I shared that it was causing cramping and not providing the intended benefit. Despite this, the recommendation was to continue the same course of treatment with minor adjustments. I expressed that I did not agree and was hoping to discuss alternative options.

At approximately 1:32, I was told my appointment time was over and that I could discontinue the medication if I chose, review materials later, and return for another visit. When I shared that I did not feel the visit had been helpful, I was told that I was being “mean,” which I found inappropriate and unprofessional.

I left the appointment feeling dismissed, unsupported, and discouraged. Patients seeking care—especially for complex and often frustrating conditions like perimenopause—deserve to feel heard, respected, and partnered with in their treatment decisions

Avoid at all costs; Don't be fooled by the Amazon branding.. To summarize:

1. I was already happy with my existing medical coverage in Florida; I only joined One Medical because of its associated Amazon branding. As a happy Amazon customer of many years, continually impressed with its exceptional customer support, I wanted to give One Medical a try in support of the Amazon brand and its mission to help improve primary care. I never would have joined One Medical had the "Amazon" name not been attached to it.

2. Prior to joining One Medical, I verified with the One Medical sales rep that my medical insurance, AvMed Entrust, was accepted.

3. Furthermore, the One Medical website specifically listed "AvMed Entrust" as an accepted insurance plan.

4. Even though One Medical bills by group, not specific physician, I proactively chose a PCP within One Medical that was showing as 'In-Network' on the AvMed website, out of an abundance of caution.

5. Mythili Penugonda, MD, an available One Medical provider, was showing as "in-network" on the AvMed website.

6. Despite joining One Medical in October with available appointments that same month, I proactively waited on scheduling my virtual visit until November to ensure that Dr. Penugonda would be my AvMed PCP of record effective 01 November.

7. Days prior to my virtual visit, I uploaded my AvMed Entrust insurance card to my One Medical profile and was informed that I had a $100 copay.

8. On 03 November 2025, Dr. Penugonda (an internal medicine physician) and I had an approximately 15- to 20-minute virtual/remote visit via Zoom. It was just a conversation, and no procedures were performed (virtual appt).

9. The $100 copay was paid promptly after my initial virtual visit with Dr. Mythili Penugonda on 03 November 2025. 

10. After the visit was completed and my copay was made, I was then told by both One Medical and AvMed that the visit was "Out of Network."

11. There is nothing more I could have reasonably done as a consumer to ensure that the visit was in-network.

12. I would never knowingly see an out-of-network physician, especially since I have insurance and can walk down the road to my prior PCP, Dr. Bharath Radhakrishna, MD, at very low cost.

13. Images in support of the statements made herein were all provided within my One Medical message portal.

14. One Medical is pursuing an additional $204 from me, which I will never pay.

15. I am now in the process of reaching out to Amazon corporate for resolution. If unsuccessful, I will cancel my prime membership
