Zero Stars: A Masterclass in Incompetence. This was hands down the most unprofessional and disorganized experience I’ve ever had with a healthcare provider. I signed up through Amazon for a free trial and scheduled a virtual visit for a UTI. After the visit, the nurse practitioner gave me a care plan that required me to go to a One Medical location for a urine sample (or another lab if I messaged them back with a location) no antibiotic prescription in the meantime, even though I was in clear discomfort. I sent a follow-up message with a few questions almost immediately, only to be told that the NP I spoke to wouldn’t return for two weeks. Another NP eventually replied, but the delay already spoke volumes about how disconnected and inefficient this service is.

When I arrived at the One Medical office, I told the receptionist I was there for a urinalysis. I asked for an estimate of the cost, which turned into a ten-minute ordeal. She finally quoted me a price of "six sixty" and I relied six hundred and sixty dollars? No, she meant $6.60 for the dipstick test but couldn’t tell me the price for the urine culture, even after several more minutes of trying. Then the lab tech came out and told me there was no provider on-site and that she couldn’t perform the dipstick test because it’s considered a “hands-on” procedure. The culture, I was told, would take 3–5 days to process. So not only was I in pain and waiting days for incomplete testing, I was also stuck in a ridiculous loop of poor communication and zero accountability.

When I asked who I could speak to about my experience, the lab tech refused to give me any contact information. I was told to “submit a message through the app” and that they would “flag” my record. That’s it. No help, no empathy, no professionalism.

From start to finish, both Amazon and One Medical completely dropped the ball. What should have been a straightforward, supportive healthcare experience turned into a wasted afternoon, unnecessary frustration, and no resolution to my actual health concern. I would never recommend this service to anyone! Ever

My name is Hanna, and I’m a physician assistant. Thanks for reaching out, and I’m happy to share information about next steps. I’ve also included general information about the virtual services that are included with your membership for ongoing support of your health and well-being. YOUR PRESCRIPTION REQUEST(S) On-demand care options, like our Discuss Common Conditions (Treat Me Now) and urgent video chat features, are great for short-term medications and medications that can be safely managed without close monitoring. Because …….. is used to treat a long-term health condition, I recommend contacting the provider who usually prescribes your medication for a refill, even if you’ve been taking the medication for a long time. If you don’t currently have a primary care provider, please let me know and I’d be happy to provide resources to help you locate a provider in your area. If you’re unable to reach your usual provider and would like us to consider sending a one-time, short-term prescription to last until you’re able to follow up with them, please answer the following questions for each requested medication: 1.	Please list the medication name, dose, and full instructions as printed on your prescription label and attach a photo of the label, if possible. 2.	What is the reason you were originally prescribed this medication? 3.	When did you start taking this medication? 4.	Who usually prescribes this medication for you (name and specialty), and when was your last visit with this provider? 5.	Could you tell me more about why you’re requesting a prescription today (e.g., unable to reach your usual provider, looking for a new provider)? 6.	Have you ever experienced any side effects? If so, please describe. 7.	When did you have your ……. last checked? Please attach a copy of your most recent results, if available. 8.	Please list any chronic or ongoing medical conditions you’ve been diagnosed with. Best, Hanna

To Whom It May Concern at One Medical,. To Whom It May Concern at One Medical,
I’m not sure it’s possible to feel more dismissed, more gaslit, or more infuriated by a healthcare provider than I have been by One Medical.
Let me be blunt: I waited nearly two years for One Medical to have an actual doctor available in-office. My condition is complex. I respect physician assistants and nurse practitioners—but in my case, I needed a doctor. And for two years, One Medical failed to provide one.
Fast forward to now: I attempted to refill a common antibiotic for diverticulitis, a condition I’ve been diagnosed with and treated for since 2022. One Medical told me they had no record of my diagnosis. None. Not the specialist referral. Not the tests. Not the prescriptions. Not the consults. Nothing.
This is beyond absurd. I saw a One Medical doctor five weeks ago who specifically prescribed medication for my diverticulitis. We had a full consult about my history with it. Yet when I called to renew that prescription, I was told—again—that there was no record of my condition, my medication, or any reason to refill it.
From August 25th to August 30th, I spent hours trying to get through to patient relations. Every call ended the same way: denial, deflection, and a suggestion to “just book another appointment.” That might work for someone in perfect health. But for me, dragging myself into an office just to refill a prescription I’ve already been given is not just inconvenient—it’s cruel.
And the worst part? I was treated like a nuisance. Every customer service rep, every clinical nurse I spoke to acted like I was irrational for asking them to do their job: to look at my records, to acknowledge my diagnosis, and to refill a medication that’s clearly documented across my specialist network—from pulmonology to cardiology. Everyone knows I have diverticulitis. Everyone except One Medical.
So here I am, stranded. The specialist who originally diagnosed me is no longer in business. And One Medical, the company that absorbed that practice, has erased my history and left me without care.
If you’re looking for a provider that will ignore your records, deny your diagnosis, and treat you like a burden—One Medical is the place. If you value your health, your time, and your dignity, stay far away.
I hope someone at One Medical actually reads this and contacts me. But based on your track record, I won’t hold my breath.
You’ve made my medical life hell.
Sincerely,
Edwin H. Balder

Don't go there. I was looking for a new PCP when mine retired.  The closest One Medical PCP was a 2-hr drive away, but I was hopeful that she would be a good fit.  I got there early -- because according to One Medical appointments start on time -- and had to wait about 15 mins.  When I did see the doctor, she rushed through my intake.  I have a complicated medical history and expected the first visit to be comprehensive so I could receive the best INDIVIDUAL care.  When I asked about continuing a medication regime I have been on for 25 years with great success, rather than saying  she would not prescribe these medications, she hid behind fuzzy language, saying things like she "wouldn't be comfortable" prescribing these meds.  No explanation for why.  I have a Ph.D. in molecular biology, have extensively researched my medications, and probably know more about their effects and risks than she does.  After 2 weeks of going back and forth over this issue, she persisted with the fuzzy language, and I have given up.  She had no basis for refusing to prescribe what I've been on for 25 years, no explanation as to what would occur should I suddenly go off this medication.  In short, I felt that her biases were paramount in her mind, over-riding what would be the best medical care for me.  Had she been definite that she would not prescribe what I wanted at that first visit, it would have saved both of us some time and I could have started looking for a new PCP again.  I won't be renewing my One Medical membership

Healthcare is sacred. Healthcare is sacred, guided by the timeless principles of the Hippocratic Oath. There should never be a two-tiered system where access to care depends on political affiliation, beliefs, race, or religion. Nothing is more egregious than a medical professional violating that oath for ideological reasons.

Nothing is more heinous than a medical provider who violates their oath due to political beliefs.

However, the culture at One Medical, influenced by progressive ideology, has led to some providers who appear to compromise their professional oath.

I experienced this firsthand. Despite lab work that flagged multiple serious comorbidities--with critical results highlighted in red--my provider at One Medical offered no treatment plan: no medications, no referrals, no follow-up. After I expressed my frustration and filed a complaint with One Medical, they terminated my account. I am seeking legal advice to file a lawsuit against this company and its parent company, Amazon.

Misleading Trial Promises and Poor Customer Service. Patients should be aware that One Medical claims there are no charges during the 14-day trial period. However, after completing a visit scheduled during that time, patients will be billed — despite being told otherwise by One Medical staff.

A fair resolution was proposed: apply the advertised pay-per-visit fee (listed on their website as $29 to $49) and cancel the membership. Even this was declined.

Disorganized, Unskilled, and Abandons Patients When It Matters Most. I had an extremely disappointing and costly experience with One Medical. Over the course of a long-term relationship with them, I spent thousands of dollars on doctor visits, referrals, and labs, expecting at least basic care coordination and record-keeping. Instead, I found that their doctors do not maintain detailed clinical notes, and lack the expertise to manage complex or chronic conditions beyond routine visits.

Despite outside specialists formally diagnosing me with a disabling condition and recommending long-term disability, and even writing a formal letter for SSA, when it came time to complete a simple RFC (Residual Functional Capacity) form — a standard part of the disability process — One Medical refused to assist. After a long period of charging me for appointments, tests, and referrals, they ultimately abandoned me when I needed critical documentation to protect my health and livelihood.

One Medical markets itself as "care-centered," but in reality, their system is fragmented, inattentive, and not equipped to support patients with anything beyond the most basic short-term needs. I would strongly caution anyone with chronic, disabling, or complex medical issues to seek care elsewhere.

For context, my experience is related to NJ and NY offices

I was recommended a cryotherapy to remove the mole (which was in fact a cyst), and was told by One Medical staff that it would be covered 100% by my insurance. After ~15 minutes of cryotherapy with a technician, I was charged $793.58. When asked about insurance coverage, I was told that since I haven't met my deductible for the year, I need to pay 100% of the bill. 

I spent hours on the phone with One Medical billing department; basically, they had no intention of adjusting the bill, and they were not not going to follow up about the misdiagnosis. I also called Blue Shield of California (my insurance), and learned that Blue Shield has no power to adjust the bill as it came from One Medical. 

The phone call with Blue Shield was still informative, however, in that I learned that One Medical basically maxed out all possible charges for their (misdiagnosed) 15 min technician treatment. Now I'll know to avoid One Medical until they know how to correctly diagnose their patients and not misinform them about the charges

Zoom Call = $1000.00. Had a twenty minute zoom call with a NP who ultimately prescribed a sleeping med. A month later I received a bill for 750.00, which was discounted from 1000.00. I called One Medical and spoke with a very nice person who agreed the price for a visit of this nature shouldn't be 1000.00. She submitted a dispute to the coding department, which was ultimately denied. They said the 750.00 bill was accurate. Sooo.. for a twenty minute zoom call I paid 750.00. This is an absolute rip off. DO NOT put yourself in this position
Scam. Scam, virtual consultation cost

Almost completely useless for teledoc…. Almost completely useless for teledoc outside of getting a prescription medication. Nothing like saying we care about your health and wellbeing than denying a referral because you can't charge more money for an office visit

Firing One Medical after over six years as a patient.. I will be firing Eliza Singer at the San Rafael Office after three to four years with her as a PCP and I will be firing One Medical after over six years.

The icing on the cake with Eliza Singer whose formal name may be Nancy was when she wouldn't fill a prescription of a medicine I have been on a number of years, and this medicine works for me.

She referred me to a specialist and without my permission sent a referral that was worded in a way I didn't like to the specialist's office.

She didn't feel like she could manage the medicine. That may be the case.

Eliza seems to like to make referrals but is not good at it. Once she referred me to a specialist and after Googling, I determined I did not want to see that specialist at all.

Another time I asked for a referral and told her the locations that worked, and she gave me two names that were in a location that didn't work. And she gave me one name of someone inexperienced who I did not feel comfortable seeing.

Once I made an appointment with Eliza two weeks in advance and she cancelled the appointment the night before. And I made an appointment with another provider and then I wanted a follow-up appointment and Eliza scolded me that I had already had an appointment about that health issue.

Eliza has taken several leaves the last couple years including a maternity leave, a sabbatical, and probably others.  That is too much time off.  One Medical should curtail her time off.

Looking at One Medical's website on my list of medicines there is a medicine I took briefly a year or so ago and have not taken since. And there is an error on my secondary insurance.

I signed up last year. I signed up last year, as I am generally happy with most Amazon services.  However, my experience with Amazon One Medical and Amazon Pharmacy was not great.  I met with my doctor in person a few times and had a couple of virtual calls.  The Doctor (a PA) was very nice, and I liked her.  She wasn't very thorough IMO, and missed some opportunities for important blood testing/screening to help me monitor any progress I was making.  Anyway, when Amazon stopped taking insurance - without warning, that was a killer.  Their pharmacy is non-communicative and probably the worst pharmacy I've ever dealt with

Scheduling specialist visits is insane. I have been a patient at One Medical in the DC area for about 6 years. The doctors and staff in each office have been wonderful.  But administration and scheduling is ridiculous.  I had to have a colpo last year and since my PCP isn't trained I had to see a different doctor.  I have a low grade cervical lesion that needs to monitored.  I couldn't schedule through the app because she isn't my PCP, and they said she is not taking new patients.  I am NOT a new patient!  And I have a literal request from this doctor to schedule with her in my app. I get it scheduled, an early morning 7:50am appointment 25 miles away in DC (I'm out in VA).  While I'm getting ready I get a portal message that I didn't look at, because I assumed it was my bloodwork results from the week before and I get on the road because I was running late, pay a toll to go on the expressway.  So 15 to 20 minutes AFTER the portal message they call me to tell me my appointment was cancelled. Like things happen, totally get that, but WHO DOESN'T pick up the phone and call the 1st patient on the schedule immediately?  You think everyone is keeping up with portal messages?  So then when I have to reschedule, which again, can't schedule through the portal so I have to go though messaging and I get the whole you're not a patient of hers, again.  YES I AM, how many times do I have to say this, don't you people track notes in the system???? When I express my frustration, she says "emergencies are unavoidable."  I'm not frustrated over there being an emergency and cancellation!!!!!!  I'm frustrated over no one picking up the damn phone and calling me immediately so that I wouldn't have gotten on the expressway that has a $20 toll!  Then she says someone will get back to me to reschedule, but nope, I need to send another message 4 days later to get it rescheduled. 

So I finally have my appointment, and my pap came back saying my lesion is changing.  Again, have to schedule through the app for my Colpo.  Today, I get a cancellation of my appointment through the app, no phone call, just a message telling ME to call or reschedule through the app, which AGAIN, I can NOT schedule though the app.  I call, get put on hold for 15 minutes and then she can't help me or get me to the people who can.  So I have to message again. Not them message me to reschedule.  I have to message them.   

You would think that since the news of my lesion changing is concerning, they would handle this with better care. Now, everyone I have connected to has been very nice, so I can't tell if there is some level of incompetence among the admin staff or if their communication polices just suck or it's just that the system is not setup to handle specialty appointments.

I pay a fee to go to one medical for convenience, ease and consistency but these last couple months have been ridiculous.  As someone who works in IT consulting, Amazon has some of the best engineers in the country and they can't make some simple changes to the app differentiating between PCP and specialist visits?  Why do I have to jump through hoops to schedule a damn pap or colpo???  Get your shit together Amazon. You're already being targeted by boycotts, DO BETTER!

It was great before Amazon bought it. It used to be great before they got bought by Amazon. Now it’s just ok. Still happy with my PCP and didn’t have any issues with billing. But messages often get mixed up, sometimes the admin is replying and the info is doubled or doesn’t go thru at all. The visit time also got cut. It’s convenient (and offices are pretty )but not fantastic like it used to be

Mixed Review. The care and access is pretty good generally although we had several primary care physicians leave. The referrals to specialists and Urgent Care by One Medical have been excellent. However, their program for mental health “Mindset” seems to me a scam. They charged ridiculous amounts for so-called evaluations for which they did a short online interview. Luckily my insurance paid most of it. The assigned therapists canceled repeatedly on little notice, with no excuse.  It seemed that the therapists took this as an unimportant side job. The program supervisor insisted on periodic sessions to check in and charged for those sessions, despite that I had no more than one or two actual sessions with the assigned therapist so I quickly left that program. Overall, One Medical provided care with a low price tag paid for by my health insurance and a copay so the value was good. Nevertheless, I have found a nearby internist who accepts my insurance and moved my care for better access to my primary physician, who follows up more closely than One Medical. One Medical may work for you if you have health insurance that One Medical accepts as in plan

Stay far far far away from Forward. Stay far far far away from Forward. They were an absolute nightmare. No, I actually came to confirm I don’t!  Another doctor flat out told me to just go vegan, with out understanding due to my food allergies I need animal protein to maintain enough protein intake.

And for the bail in the coffin, I asked for my health records…today over a year later of this request I finally received them. So yes, stay very very clear of Forward for your own mental health

Dangerously Incompetent. Dangerously incompetent care. Stay away. Let me explain… I signed up for Forward with great hope and enthusiasm. It seemed a fantastic option for “Medicine 3.0” where technology and lifestyle management combine to help prevent the common causes of illness and injury. Core to this approach is intelligent use of blood labs to monitor a variety of bio markers that are not commonly tracked by most doctors. Not only did my 45-minute baseline visit not include MANY of these labs (I had to ask for them to be added, and the physician obliged) but when it actually came to scheduling the blood draw that’s when the REAL trouble ensued… I was told by a customer service rep before I signed up that all blood draws could be done at home since I don’t live near a physical office. NOPE. That was just a straight up lie. They don’t do that. Then I asked for lab scripts so that I could go to a Quest Diagnostics center and have the blood drawn there. It took FOUR days for them to get me the scripts. And when I got them… The scripts were for only TWO of the EIGHT tests the doctor had ordered. When I raised the problem with staff they said “oh we don’t know what happened but we’ll sort it out.” It took them another DAY to get the full battery of tests… and they were wrong AGAIN! They had erroneously replaced two critical tests with tests that were never ordered and wouldn’t be helpful. If I hadn’t been such a close reader of the lab scripts I would never have noticed this - I would have just gone to the lab and gotten the wrong tests. Would my doctor have ever noticed? I doubt it. How can you render competent, safe care when you can’t even quality control the blood labs that you are basing your care on? This service was completely inept. It’s dangerous. Stay away

Just left! Declining service is disappointing. Just left after being one of the first clients in midtown nyc. AVOID AT ALL COSTS - Massive waste of time & money. My entire goal for signing up with Forward was to establish better care for myself that was also convenient and cost effective. I’m in a worse position than when I started thanks to their “team”, I took time off work for an appointment that furnished zero positive outcome and have lost sleep many nights in a row due to the stress and anxiety they caused during this ordeal which has dragged on for weeks.

My wife and I had extremely detailed conversations over multiple phone calls and text message threads with their sales rep. We asked every question we could think of and everything was “double checked” and “confirmed” by their sales rep. Their “care team” is absolutely horrendous and responds as if they are a robot who can’t comprehend what you are saying or asking. The customer service department is apparently nonexistent as they have never reached out and I was never provided with a point of contact after multiple requests, so your stuck with their “care team” who DOESN’T CARE.

They did nothing but waste my families time and get our hopes up. They continually pushed my concerns and requests to the side without any regard to fixing their endless mistakes. 

Highly unprofessional company who grossly lacks integrity. Just another company with shady operating practices looking to line their pockets and treat you like another statistic. Stay FAR away from them, unless you are looking to waste money and never get any true support. After my experiences with them and reading other reviews; I’m not surprised they have such a bad reputation, do not become a victim of this scam of a company!!

When I went for my first visit. When I went for my first visit, they did standard blood work, that did not include my vitamin D levels. I told them I have low D and they said they would have to submit through my insurance. (I thought this was supposed to supersede insurance ?) I got a bill for $266 from Quest- when I reached out to Forward they stated it was because I requested it - not the doctor- What on earth is that?!? How can the “future of healthcare” not test for a long-standing deficiency? I had to pay on top of $150 a month- I could have gotten the vitamin d test through my provider for $25 now I am on the hook for $266-if you have insurance - stay clear  of this scam !! It is a money making scam and nothing more- I am telling everyone I know to stay away-

UPDATE- Reply to Forward- the details are - when I asked the doctor to run a full blood panel to check my thyroid and vitamin d levels - because again I have issues with them- she said that Forward did not cover them and I had to go through insurance (again what if I didn’t have insurance - how can “the future of healthcare” not cover a condition I have had half my life) - I got a bill for the vitamin D test for $266 and   When I inquired the answer was that I requested it and not the doctor. So again- why did I pay $150 a month to be told I have to go through my own insurance to then not have my insurance cover it because I requested it? It makes no sense

Dr in Seattle was terrific; infrastructure & customer service HORRIBLE!. I was terminated by Forward by their CEO & founder AFTER I complained about the defects in their mobile app and numerous broken promises to call to schedule a follow up virtual meeting with an MD in Seattle, WA who suggested ways to treat my anxiety and insomnia.  Their mobile app only listed "Follow-up" appointments with different Dr's despite the Seattle MD promising me he'd arrange for his appointment availability to be populated on my mobile app.
Forward does NOT disclose on their website that patient interaction is 100% via their mobile app. There is no patient log-in and you can NEVER reach a live person when you call any of their phone numbers. Finally, they use Alta Pharmacy which delayed my prescription after I paid with no firm delivery date. One is not able to choose a preferred pharmacy via their mobile app. Next, a supervisor spoke to me on May 6.  She sent me an email that my prescription was transferred to my preferred pharmacy & ready for pickup. That was a lie as the pharmacy told me it would not be ready till 6pm on May 8!  
The supervisor said that Forward uses a "distributed care model" (not disclosed on their website) where any Dr can pick up where previous Dr left off just by reading your medical chart.  I told her that was preposterous as many things you tell a Dr in confidence are not recorded on your chart.  Furthermore, Forward uses Dr's for telehealth from all over the U.S. and it is very doubtful they converse with one another to share important details of previous Dr-patient discussions which is NOT documented in the patient's chart.
Via email, I requested Forward's CEO to reconsider my termination & reinstate, but that email was ignored.  During our May 8 termination conversation, the CEO promised Forward would send me contact info of health care providers in my area that offered "concierge service" for a fixed monthly price.  Instead, I received an email with a list of Santa Clara county health care providers which require patients to have Medicare B or MediCal. I don't have either and had told that to the CEO.  Very disappointing!

My rating for the Forward Seattle MD I met with virtually is A+.  My rating for Forward's infrastructure and patient support/customer care is F-

When I first start using the app. When I first start using the app. it looked good but looks can be deceiving. After I ended Forward about two months ago. I then requested my medical records. Don't recommend. Update: it see FWD has replied: it’s easy, FWD: actually call Quest so they can refund my money, or you refund it!

OG review:

I wish I had come here and read these reviews before ever signing up for them - I'll tell you the bad, and then the good.

The bad- they need to stop advertising as if they cover everything: they do not.  To be able to monitor your health, labs need to happen, and they run those in-house. For whatever reason, however, they don't trust themselves and the dr's have a habit of sending everything out to Quest anyway.  Even the first time, not because they wanted to check results, but because my bloods weren't taken to the lab on time (their mistake), so one of them expired and they had to have me redo it at my own expense - that shouldn't have happened, but at the time, I had insurance, and I just rolled with it.  I stayed with them well over a year (because there is some good, like I said), and in that time we had three different insurances, and also at some points NO INSURANCE.  While w/o insurance they took my blood and sent the whole thing to Quest without even checking with me!  The bill was a whole $332 extra - this is in addition to the fact that they charge you every month regardless of whether you see them or not - which, if they really did take care of everything, would be fine - but again: they don't - they actually create additional expenses.  I was so appalled at this, and told them as much - they assured me they would call Quest to see what could be done with the bill.  So I trustingly waited, only to get another bill reminder for the same amount, now saying, of course, that I'm past due. I told them this, and they just said they'd check on the call to Quest (lies, I now realize).  I'm a very prompt bill-payer, and I don't like leaving bills unpaid, so now I was also a bit uneasy.  Ultimately, I left them, more details on that in a second, but today I'm calling Quest myself to find out what the update is and take care of these bills (plural, because FORWARD did this once more, and in fact I had a new bill for another $75) - and guess what? FORWARD NEVER CALLED THEM!!! There is a way that Forward could have called and applied a UPP on the requisition of the bill to help me out, but they DID NOT - the lady on the phone checked several times because she (Quest) wanted to help and make sure I wasn't paying more than I needed to, but FORWARD NEVER CALLED EVER - Quest could apply a discount for me IF THEY HAD RECEIVED A CALL FROM FORWARD.  Quest has zero record of Forward even calling, never mind doing anything on the call, but they didn't even pick up the phone to call like they said they would.  I'm just... shocked.  

In the meantime, between the first bill and me finally leaving them, it's time for me to do a follow up, so I go to do so.  Turns out my dr is gone!  And the other dr I'd seen way back was unavailable for MONTHS.  All they could offer was some new dude in the ATL office.  As many other people out there, I prefer a dr of my own gender.  (I'm sure the new dr is lovely) So basically, they can't even take care of their patients.   It's worth mentioning that in my time with them I saw three different drs, mainly because they didn't have, and never did get, their act together.  This is a failure to deliver on their promise - essentially they don't deliver on any of the reasons that they could supposedly be a better care system for anyone - certainly not affordably.  It's kind of ludicrous and extra to be paying more than you ever would for what you actually need, it's not like anyone will choose to go see a dr even every month just for the fun of it (I almost would have for the nice food bars they have, but... uh, no - I have a life)- so this is all very extra - but if we choose to go ahead and luxe up our health care, the very least they could do is actually follow through on their promises.  As far as I can tell, they are categorically failing to do so on several points. 

I promised some good: 
The dr's were overall very caring. Ultimately, not a single one actually landed on what would help lower my blood pressure (turning to a WFPBNO lifestyle), and one actually doubted it was the right path, but they did support the changes I made on my own, at least, and helped me monitor the progress, and were glad to be working with me towards taking me off the med I had started needing.  The medical assistants are downright sweet - lovely and wonderful, I looked forward to seeing them, really.  And they have the best treats - that are actually WFPBNO! I looked forward to grabbing a couple of those food bars, too.  The experience was pleasant.  However: it is not ground-breakingly different - the dr's didn't even land on what actually worked- and it is certainly not worth what they are charging, and their failure to even follow up on their promises means I will never again return to them.  EVER.  I'm out an extra $400+, but glad to be done with this scam

Absolutely the worst service ever!!. Absolutely the worst service ever!!! The infamous "Chat" that Everyone has been complaining about with forward health is accurate. Please read the negative reviews they are all true. The app was is only functional about 30% of the time. Because if they have told you they resolve something and you notice it was not resolved they will just stop responding to you because they don’t know how to help you and don’t want to take the time to get involved in something that requires more time and thought. Many people just cancel all together without even getting care.  it is not, as forward has explained in all of their marketing and promotional campaigns, a Concierge Medical Experience that they offer. I wanted Forward to be everything they…. I wanted Forward to be everything they said, and I was very optimistic, first they had issues drawing blood, then I couldn’t even get to the virtual baseline because I don’t have a PC and they said my IPad Pro or IPhone 12 Pro wouldn’t work? It seems outdated. How is that? They’re suppose to be “futuristic” using the latest technologies. I use these devices with social media, zoom and talking to my family often via video chat. When I canceled I got no response of cancellation, I found out by not being able to log in. I’m still unsure if they canceled my membership properly. Waste of time and money

The most frustrating elements of both high tech and US medicine.. Forward is everything that is wrong with big tech and big medicine all rolled up in one. I was there 6 months and was ignored every single time I asked to have baseline appointment and cancer screening, two advertised services. I was nagged incessantly by the app to send in my DNA sample. There is nothing medically useful at the $99 level of 23andme, unless there's some medical connection to why one finds cilantro distasteful, or where one's ancestors lived.  When I ended my membership, the app was instantly disabled, despite promises it wouldn't be - ostensibly so I could provide "valuable feedback." My bloodwork and other data - that belongs to me - is unavailable.  My data is Forward's now, to use for their profit. I'm sure it's a violation of HIPPAA for them to sell it, but I don't have confidence in a Google alumnus not to take advantage ("act first, apologise later") The app is, or was, between May and October 2022, terrible. Most information is in a 3 or 4 point font, same color, but a shade lighter, than the background. There are no accessibility settings, including zooming, adjusting type size, color or transparency. Most iOS apps can be enlarged. Forward ignores iOS accessibility settings.  Then there's the chat. Never the same "person". I was chided for asking if I were chatting with a bot. I thought it was a fair question. Here's an example. I had reported, let's say, a headache the day before. (Details slightly changed.) The next day there was the usual followup. "How's your headache?" Me: "The pain is gone." Mlle pas un robot: "Have you tried Tylenol?" Maybe it was human, but it was emulating at best a cut-rate call center. The app constantly nags and keeps pressuring customer (I use that word on purpose) to make an appointments for some symptom. This will be a voice call from someone the customer hasn't met and whose credentials she can't look up. My spam blocker wouldn't let it ring through, because their caller ID was  the main Forward HQ number that no one answers, ever. (Also, one cannot call the local office.) Apparently my spam blocker considered this a robocaller. The doctor or nurse probably using their personal cell phone, kept getting blocked, leaving a voicemail, but always the robot callback number. A normal medical office has at least, monitored voicemail. I couldn't contact anyone to ask for a number to call,or to put in my contacts that wouldn't be blocked. So I never had my phone consult. No regrets. I prefer not to talk to a medical person over every symptom. The system is automated beyond ridiculous. If this is the future of medicine, it's "Idiocracy." I managed one Zoom appointment which resulted in a prescription I had  tried that I explained, did not work. The MD insisted that was what I needed anyway. This wasn't a consultation, a collaboration between doctor and patient. Rather, it was a template. I had one in person appointment (NOT the relaxing, no-time-limits baseline appointment they advertise) after reporting a possibly serious symptom and sending a photo. This resulted, eventually (I went back to my real primary physician) in a referral to a specialty practice.The worst part was the constant notifications and frustrating chats with either bots or non-English speakers, or maybe a combination of the two -- if human, clicking a response to a keyword. The founder of Forward, a former Google executive, says, "Medicine should be a product, not a service." That gave it away, and Forward is precisely that. The product he is selling is an expensive subscription app, which, like a game, keeps the player engaged. Every once in a while, there is a payoff, but it's not worth the time invested or the price. He truly doesn't provide a service. A shopping app provides a service, but the app is not the point for either party.It provides connection. Forward's app is the end product. The consumer is paying to interact daily with a poorly thought out app. There's little real service (but then, they're not selling a service). Their office in my city is in a very high-end (Cartier, Hermés) shopping area with limited expensive parking. If you overstay, and don't run out and feed the meter, your car will be booted, which will cost a great deal of time and money. If you have a mobility problem, I guess you have to call an Uber (where another founder came from). I fervently hope the US medical system is overhauled, but Forward is the wrong direction - pun not intended. Forward is just another low-overhead, high profit,glitzy but superficial fever dream from Silicon Valley. I don't expect it to be around in a couple of years

I was told I could take the skin tests and other tests they had wanted me to do there only to discover I’d have to come back, over an hour drive, to complete. Which means sleeping in parking lots waiting for my brain to operate again.  

I am now without medications I require to operate, I am losing contracts weekly because I cannot focus to do or stay awake to follow through and I came to this service because I am desperate for a doctor to simply continue therapy and medications that I have already been through hell of trial and error to discover works.

On top of this, the doctor who did my baseline appointment and ordered blood tests told me they were covered in my membership. In a fish tank. There is a reply from Forward below stating they would contact me by email.  The message was sent here on October 12.  It has been two weeks since there message and I still have not received an email from Forward.

Android app integration.
Android app integration, fitness tracking, sleep tracking, etc. does not exist.  I joined Forward over a year ago (August 2019) and during the initial sales call I was told that the Android app integration was coming soon.  Over a year later (October 2020) I was told again that the Android app integration is still "coming soon."  I'm not sure how Forward defines "coming soon" but to me, taking more than a year is not "coming soon."  Taking over a year and not having any Android app integration is basically a "pants on fire" level lie.

The Fish tank.  
When you first enter the Forward office in DC you enter a glass fish tank reception area where you will get your initial screening.  In public, you'll be weighed and measured as people pass you on the sidewalk watching you.  If the angle is just right a passerby will be able to see all of your data, your weight, your pulse, your blood oxygen level, etc.  In a typical doctor's office before you are weighed and measured you have a chance to take off your coat, shoes, put down your wallet or purse, your cell phone, to get an accurate weight.  Forward doesn't give you that chance.  Trying to take off your shoes, coat, wallet, phone, etc. is super awkward while you're in the fish tank, with only two chairs, no privacy, and passersby watching.  Also, there's no private place to store your wallet or purse, phone, or other valuable items.  Instead you just have to leave your valuables on a little coffee table, behind you where you can't watch them, as your initial screening is happening.  Any opportunist could run in steal them and run out.  There's no security in the front room to stop them and I don't think the receptionist is going to tackle the thief.
	
Genetic Risk Analysis.  
It takes much longer than advertised on the website.  They have you take a 23andMe test kit home, take the test on your own time, and send it to 23andME for analysis.  The initial analysis from 23andMe is fast and your results come quickly.  Then you find out from Forward that you have to email them the full genome analysis from 23andMe.  You have to request it wait a while and get the initial analysis.  Now you have to send that genome analysis to Forward for analyzation by your doctor.  Then you wait, and wait, and wait some more.  Finally, after a long time (months in my case) your doctor will tell you they are ready to have an appointment with you to review your genetic analysis.  You would think that since Forward is a modern tech startup they could just upload the data into your app and you could review it on your own time and if you have any questions, you could schedule an appointment but that is not the case.  You have to see the doctor before they'll give you your data.  Remember it’s your data not theirs and they will hold it hostage until you do what they want.  This doesn't happen with just the genetic analysis, it happens with all the test results, cholesterol, insulin levels, etc.  The pattern for me was always take this test then you have to do this thing (schedule an appointment) before we will give you the results.
	
Good part:
If you needed to schedule a test for something or get a medication refilled the people responding in the app were generally responded very quickly

Sad there is so little return on investment with Forward.. June 3, 2024
I’m leaving Forward — invested too much $$ already. 
And here is why:


1. App functionality is a major issue also. 

When I first joined F in 2019, they said an IPad app was in the works. 
Every time I asked about it over the years, they said they were working on it, it was coming soon. 
It never happened. 

Reading teeny san-serif grey font on a white background is very difficult. 

Trying to work thru majors problems by chat is difficult, when a phone call would be so much easier. 

2. 
The fact that Forward doesn’t cover labs that are important decreases value. 
You don’t cover A1c — even tho this test CAN be done in office — nor Vitamin D, fasting insulin, not even my CBC. W/ diff — despite a documented blood problem.  

I’m paying $150 / month for WHAT? These tests don’t cost that much, wholesale, and only need to be done every 6-12 months, by which time I’ve paid $900 - $1800!

Your genetic testing doesn’t include APO-e or MTHFR genes. 

Yet Forward promotes its services as being for the data-nerds, the people who care about being proactive about their health. … But doesn’t cover pro-active testing as stated above. 

In December 2023, When I had what I thought was strep throat, initially I couldn’t get an in office visit for NINE days!  That’s crazy!   And then the first visit had to be via app — with me trying to take pictures of my throat! How do you do a virtual visit for strep throat?

3.  Way too many problems with coordinating referrals / appointments  with other facilities. The George Washington Hospital referral never worked after months of going back and forth about it. 

4.  Way too many problems with getting prescriptions refilled in a timely way with my mail order pharmacy — mainly having to do with the doctor’s prescriber number the: scheduled drugs. 

5.  Way too long to it lab results in app. 

6. Of the virtual visits I had scheduled, more than half (2/3) never worked (tech issues).  And virtual visits can only be done in laptop, not phone.  And of course not on IPad, since there’s no IPad app. 

I’ve paid $150/ month for all these years and have gotten little in return. 

I did get PAP smear and skin scans done in office. 
The in-office staff (both MAs and the licensed providers)  were great. 

But even there, in clinic, the complimentary water changed from the good stuff to warehouse- quality water.   The toilet room towels changed from good ones, more high end, to cheapy thin paper towels. 

The fancy body scanner upon admission … the temperature system doesn’t work. So overall, it’s just a flashy way to check weight and O2 saturation. 

I could go on, but for now I’ll stop. 
I had high hopes about Forward.  Very little came true

GoForward Medical:flashy but fails in execution. May 20, 2024
While generally pleased with the professional (Ie. licensed provider .and med tech staff) care with Forward, and the general in-clinic experience (lots of bells and whistles, and niceties, such as a bottle of water )... overall, the admin side and follow up has declined beyond my liking. 

Follow up is abysmal: 
** You never know who you are interacting with:  a desk person or a professional provider. 
**Increasingly over the past  4.5 years, many questions have gone unanswered, refills not filled in a timely way, repeated requests for clarification have been delayed by days and weeks, appointment cards are filled out incorrectly, requests via the app and via email to talk with a real person by phone have been ignored, 
** Then there was the lab billing snafu:

In Aug. 2023, I had a number of very standard tests drawn, all but one of one of which were going to be paid by Forward.  (One unusual test was required by an outside agency for something else, and I knew i'd have to pay for that one.)  
All the other tests were follow-up for ongoing problems.  In early December 2023 (4 months after!) the lab said my account was Past Due.  That was a surprise to me.  I wrote Forward immediately, requesting payment. Over the next 3 months, including the holidays, they dithered about not being able to contact Quest labs.  In the meantime, MY credit was being dinged!  Overall, this was quite stressful.

Finally in late Feb., after at least a dozen text exchanges, Forward told me that they could not cover the tests bc of paperwork??   So I got stuck with almost $700 worth of tests, for which they offered me 2 month free services.  
We finally negotiated 2 more months, and even then they still tried to charge me until I caught it and protested. 

Today they say they can no longer cover any lab tests unless done in house (and the ones done in house are quite minimal!)  
They never communicated this change to their patients.  They do not even do in-house A1c tests, which are very easy to do and not expensive.   For the $150/month fee i'm paying, standard tests should be covered. 

In early Dec. 2023, I became ill.  My throat developed soreness with a pain score of 8+.  I figured I had strep throat. Couldn't get an appt for FIVE days!   Finally after arguing, they squeezed me in the next day. The rapid strep in the office was negative.  They wanted a Covid test, even tho I had no standard covid symptoms. But they were going to send out that test, which would have cost me a lab fee!  So I declined that test. 
(Upon my return home, I )took a home covid test, which turned out positive. My symptoms were very not standard for covid!)

In January 2024, @NateFavini, (the head of Forward, sent out an email requesting a positive review.  I did not submit any review at that time, but did reply directly to him, requesting a talk about the continued problems I was having with Forward.  No one every replied. 

At least two virtual visits failed, even tho I had logged into the system and was waiting for the doc to show up.  The connection never was made.  

I do not at all recommend Forward -- they have not been cost-effective for me and the service overall is poor

Good until they’re not.. I started out with Forward a few years ago on the $99.00 intro and actually liked the service up until the end.  Despite not being the most convenient location for me, only 21 miles door to to door but could take up to an hour and half with traffic to get there, I stuck it out.  I really like the overall experience in the office and the doctors are adequate but they seem to change quite often.  Not sure if there’s a retention problem or not but I have been through several different doctors over the course of my time with them.  Using the app is easy and getting prescriptions filled was always simple and convenient.  

My first problematic experience was last year which was instigated by a faulty BP monitor.  My readings were coming in high so the doctor put me on a diuretic. This caused my BP to tank and I became quite dehydrated ending up in the ER.  The ER doc took me off the diuretic.  It just happened that I was scheduled to see my cardiologist and in checking my blood pressure which was normalized asked if I was sure the monitor was working correctly. Turns out it wasn’t, but how was I to know without being asked to check against a different unit.  

What ended my relationship with Forward started with my recent experiences this past month.  I caught COVID and was able to get paxlovid prescribed right away.  Appreciated that.  I needed some follow up bloodwork.  While stuck in traffic, I was afraid I might be late for my appointment time so I notified the company though the app. Their response was that they have a 15 minute grace period and if I couldn’t make it I would have to reschedule.  I’m already going out of my way for a simple blood test that could be accomplished by Quest 5 minutes from my house so this was not an acceptable response.  Fortunately I made it within their”window” and everything was fine.  A week later I started having debilitating lower back pain as a result of the covid so I reached out to forward right away.  Their response was essentially I could schedule a tele meeting with a doctor with the first available date almost 2 weeks away.  Completely unacceptable.  I messaged back that I needed something right away and their response was they would get back to me.  I was ready to head to the ER but decided to try teledoc through my insurance.  Got an online video appointment within 10 minutes and he called in a prescription I was able to get within 30 minutes. No charge for the call and less than $5.00 for the prescription.  Problem solved. Would have been very simple to keep me as a member but now I’m gone and will unfortunately steer anyone thinking of joining away.  Lots of new competition coming their way in this space so I don’t see this model working in the long run if they can’t take care of their paying members when members need them the most.  Very disappointing

Not really worth it. Not really worth it. I joined to get a more concierge approach to my health care. It’s boilerplate at best. Not really worth the money if you already have health care

I don't expect them to replace hospitals and medical specialists, but I expected them to give me care worth paying them on top of my regular medical provider, and I definitely didn't get anything close to it. Boutique Medicine. Im just not that ill that i can justify paying 150$ a month to see a doctor. I would also be shocked you could get in as “often” as you want in a month. 
Its basically “boutique healthcare.” With boutique healthcare if your being admitted to the Hospital for a medical illness you most likely be admitted by the Medical Residents (if its a Residency Hospital) because your considered “self pay,” not using Insurance. Most “boutique” healthcare Doctors use Hospitals that have Medical Residents so they can manage the patient (yourself) and your Doctor doesn't get bothered late at night or weekends etc.  I’ve worked in the ER Dept in Residency program for Medical, Surgical and Radiology,Level 1 Trauma Center/ Hospital for 21 yrs. So i am very familiar with “boutique” care. 
Patients being admitted to the Hospital for medical, not surgical issues (diabetes, DKA, back pain, cirrhosis etc are admitted by a Doctor “on call” for your primary Insurance group
(Blue Cross, Aetna, Cigna). 
Back in mid 2000s the position of “Hospitalist” was created, this is a Physician who only admits patients to the Hospital based on their insurance group. They work for a Insurance provider and they only manage Hospital inpatients (no private office visits). Intensivist manage patients in ICU, SICU (Critical Care Admits), Cardiologist  admit patients to CCU or step down telemetry for Cardiac issues (Open Heart, to Afib) for your insurance group. The Medical Residents admit any patient who does not have/ or use insurance or pays cash, like Forward. These patients are considered “self pays.”
So you pay $150 a month to have a Resident Admit you to the Hospital and manage your care. There is nothing wrong with Residents their in Hospital 24/7 and are immersed in seeing sick patients, but i want the guy im paying $150 (minimum) to come in and manage my care

Disappointed. The reason I was so excited about this preventative program was the Body Scanner video in the infomercial.   However, when I signed up, I was not told that there was no Body Scanner at the San Jose location.  There was something that looked like a Body Scanner, but it could not even record my heart rate. Their whole premise is great but not the real thing. Their whole premise is great, until you need them and it’s a never ending back and forth with your care team to finally get a prescription or an appointment card.

My overall evaluation is: they are not worth $150 a month. They have no same day appointments and they want you to see the same doctor even when they don’t have time to see you.

Very inconsistent experience. The majority of the doctor interaction was great. That said, all communication needs to be via the App Chat feature which is terrible. Whoever is the "Care Team" in the App Chat doesn't read all information provided, keeps asking the same question over and over, and repetitively drop the ball which has led to delayed prescription refill, incorrect dosages or lack of prior authorization request follow ups

The good, the bad and the ugly. There is no waiting room. They do the the scan basically in a window front where everyone walking by in the mall can see your vitals and weight, which made me very uncomfortable. Isn't this a violation of HIPAA? I understand that it is a marketing tactic, but patients should be given the option from the first visit to use the machine they have behind closed doors. I expressed my concern when I saw the Dr and they said they would note my chart and use the machine in the back for future visits.

The Dr I saw in person was super nice and voiced her every move which made me very comfortable.  I liked how fast their basic labs results were available in about an hour. I had a test sent out to a lab that they have a contract with for discounted lab fees. Upon receiving the results, there was something indicative that I may need antibiotics so I told them to just send the script just in case, but they said NO and wanted me to do another out of pocket test with the lab they are affiliated with.  I called my regular GP who said this is ridiculous and she sent me the medication.  She said the test Forward wanted me to get was a waste of time and money based on the original results and them saying it "may go away on it's own" was far fetched and it would have been more proactive to write the script. Obviously this is just another ploy to send more business to Quest and waste everyone's time and more money.

It appears Forward has partnered up with Quest and 23andMe which is good and bad. Good in the sense that Quest supposedly gives you a discount on their lab fees, I guess I'll find out when I get the bill and compare it to their price list.  They only use 23andMe to take your raw DNA data although they can get the same raw data from Ancestry in the same manner. Do you see where I'm going with this? (This is why there's a direct link from the forward app to 23andMe) I previously uploaded my raw data from Ancestry to Genetic Genie and Promeatheas before joining Forward and got the same results when I recently uploaded my 23andMe. Now I have to wait 3 weeks for Forward's virtual appointment to get their genetic analysis of my 23andMe although I already know which variants I have, but curious how they will approach it.

Forward can't handle all my meds like my GP, which makes no sense of even getting this membership. The Dr from my baseline visit suggested I try a different medication, which was an epic failure for me and no one has even contacted me to follow up to see how I was doing on the med.  Crazy right?

My overall opinion of Forward is they use techy and gimmicky tactics to entice you to join. I can get the same care from my GP (minus instant labs) or even an online Dr (minus med management) for a fraction of the cost.  Depending on how my next appointments go, will decide whether I will continue with their membership and will update my review accordingly

Scammed me out of a entire year's membership because I was diagnosed with cancer. I was about 80% of the way through my one-year Parsley Health membership—which included five visits with a health coach and five with a doctor—when I was diagnosed with cancer. My life was completely turned upside down. After undergoing multiple surgeries, I realized my one-year membership was about to expire, so I reached out to ask what options were available to me.

I misread a somewhat confusing email response and believed they were advising me to schedule my remaining appointments before the one-year window closed. I did exactly that and completed the full ten visits - the ones I had already paid for.

Afterward, I discovered that Parsley Health had charged me for a second year of membership, which I neither wanted nor needed. Although they applied a small discount, I was still charged hundreds of dollars for a second year of care that I did not use. I only attended the appointments included in my original membership.

When I raised this issue, I was told they had to charge me because “they are a business.” If a healthcare company cannot show flexibility or compassion for someone going through cancer, it is not worth working with

I was a Parsley Health member for 2…. I was a Parsley Health member for 2 years, drawn to their holistic approach to healthcare. Unfortunately, the reality fell far short of what's promised for a premium healthcare membership.

Major Issues:
Provider Continuity Problems: I had two different doctors leave during my membership, forcing me to restart my care from scratch each time - rebuilding relationships, re-explaining my complete health history, and re-establishing treatment goals. Critical information gets lost in these transitions. My most recent doctor completely forgot to order my lipid panel despite cholesterol monitoring being a primary reason I joined Parsley. A provider who truly knew my health profile would never miss something so fundamental to my care plan.

Scheduling Difficulties: Consistently struggled to get timely appointments. I later learned this was partly due to understaffing issues - unacceptable when you're paying premium prices for healthcare access.

Administrative Problems: Multiple billing errors, credits not properly applied, and incomplete lab orders requiring follow-up corrections.

The Final Straw: When my membership was ending, a team member reached out asking for feedback and offering a 15% discount. I sent a detailed, thoughtful response outlining all these issues and asking if a more substantial offer might be possible given the service failures. I never received a response.
This non-response perfectly encapsulates Parsley's core problem: lack of genuine follow-through and member care. When a patient takes time to provide honest feedback about their healthcare experience, complete silence speaks volumes about how much the company actually values its members.

Parsley Health has a compelling philosophy and approach, but the execution - particularly around provider stability and consistent care delivery - makes it impossible to recommend at this price point. You're paying premium prices for what ends up being fragmented, inconsistent care

I struggled with significant fatigue and after using Parsley for a year, did not gain any value whatsoever. Partitioners suggested checking for mold testing, gut health, etc etc. I consider myself very informed on functional health, while not an expert or a medical professional. Their recommendations are straight from a google search - all of these things are very common functional areas to evaluate. You do not need to pay parsley for such advice. Furthermore, one practitioner suggested I read a random book that had nothing to do with my issues. This is the kind of fake shenaniganry Parsley will provide you as health recommendations. The health coaches are also a complete joke - they will recommend similarly useless things. I seriously question most practitioners are qualified to provide holistic, functional care in a field that is rapidly, evolving with continuous research one needs to deeply understand and stay on top of. Furthermore, I'm highly skeptical the company itself treats its staff well. I recommend spending your money on your own independent blood testing, doing your own research (follow reddit threats, start to build your understanding of your body).  Since working on my health independently, I am much more in tune with my body. I understand how my energy levels fluctuate, what was affecting my low cortisol and weight gain. And I'm on the path to true healing, and already feel MUCH better than I did with any of their recommendations. You can expect nothing more than an ineffective one size fits all playbook from Parsley

Felt like a total scam. Avoid.. I booked Sesame to get a prescription for B12 injections, which is a routine request, with a documented history of low B12 that I explained to their "physician" up front. He never asked to see a single lab. Never reviewed my records. Never engaged with any specific of my case. He issued a blanket refusal before looking at anything.

What's more, this "physician" seemed to not understand basic human biology, and the two reasons he gave were both flatly wrong. First, that B12 is "a GI issue" needing a gastroenterologist — it isn't; B12 injections for deficiency and pernicious anemia are routine primary care that GPs handle every day. Second, that he couldn't "verify or modulate" the dose — there is nothing to modulate; injectable B12 comes in standardized ampoules at fixed, well-established doses. B12 has no known toxic dose and is sold over the counter without a prescription in many different countries for this reason.

So, TLDR, I paid for an individualized medical assessment and received a pre-formed "no" from someone who didn't read my file. When I asked for a refund, Sesame refused. In my opinion that is indistinguishable from a scam: charge for a consultation, deliver nothing of clinical value, then keep the money. I would not use them again and would warn anyone considering it to spend their money somewhere a provider will actually look at your case

DO NOT USE THE COSTCO FERTILITY SERVICE THROUGH SESAME. Do not use the fertility service through the Costco partnership

First - I will start by saying the doctor and the customer service team were AMAZING - quick and responsive. This is a Sesame corporate issue. 

Here’s the problems:

1. The first step in IVF planning when using a donor or not - which most do - is getting a genetic carrier screen. The doctor through Sesame wrote me a prescription for this lab work. Quest would not accept this since it wasn’t through the portal. The doctor confirmed she was unable to enter the CPT code (which I then confirmed with both Sesame and Quest) was an issue because Sesame does not allow their own providers access to genetic carrier screening CPT codes. So they are unable to handle the most basic function of fertility bloodwork which is step 1. This is not a well thought out vertical of business and it screams money grab based on their marketing vs actual capabilities. 

2. The initial doctor you meet is not an IVF specialist (they are mostly family medicine PAs) - their job is to write the prerequisite scans and bloodwork prescriptions (most of which Sesame does not have the capability for - see #1 above).  They then refer you out to their partnered IVIRMA locations which are the actual IVF clinics. PLEASE DO YOUR RESEARCH ON WHERE THESE CLINICS ARE. They are only in 10 states according to their website with most states having few locations most which are nowhere near patients. 

These are very large, established corporations in partnership. The program is not sufficient and from what I’ve seen alludes to a money grab of multiple groups making money from the 3 layers of referrals (Costco, Quest, Sesame and IVIRMA) without proper capabilities for the service they claim to provide

THIS IS MY PLEA TO SESAME CARE:. THIS IS MY PLEA TO SESAME CARE:
I have a simple request and that is to receive my prescription at a location where I will be able to receive it in a safe and timely manner. I need my prescription delivered on a weekday (Monday through Friday) to my office.  This is how I got my prescription for many months before I paid for a full year up front. Now that you have my money, you totally ignore my request. 

When you insisted on delivering it on a Saturday, I was forced to change my delivery to my home. Unfortunately, after waiting all day (and wasting my one day off) FedEx never showed up. After hours of stress due to angry phone calls to FedEx, they finally brought it at 8:00 PM. If they had not shown up on Saturday evening, they would not have delivered it until Monday, when I would not be home to receive it. In addition, the meds would have been out of refrigeration for five days.

I take my prescription early on Saturday morning so that side effects won't affect me on Sunday at church or Monday through Friday at work. Receiving it on Saturday evening interferes with my schedule.

Why is this such a difficult request for you to honor?

THIS IS THEIR ANSWER:
If you would like your medication to be delivered to your office, it is essential that you update the delivery address on your end prior to the shipping of your next medication order. Regarding the delivery time, we cannot guarantee specific days for arrival. Once we have handed the package over to the courier, we will not have visibility into its movement. However, you are welcome to reach out to the courier directly for further assistance regarding your delivery request.

As you can plainly see, they refuse to help out

Bait-and-Switch With Sesame Plus: No 90-Day Refills, Poor Customer Service. I recently had a highly disappointing experience with Sesame's customer service, specifically regarding their Sesame Plus membership. The membership advertises 90-day prescription refills with no listed exclusions, which was a major factor in my decision to sign up. However, when I sought a refill for a standard, non-narcotic medication I’ve been taking for years—after consulting a provider through their mental health program—I was told I could only receive 30-day refills and would need to schedule monthly appointments.

When I questioned why Sesame Plus was promoted as offering 90-day refills if that was not the case, I never received a clear answer. The customer service representative was unhelpful and even ended the conversation abruptly. When I called back, the phone agent didn’t seem to know what Sesame Plus was or how to verify my membership at all.

This feels like a classic bait-and-switch. The promises made about Sesame Plus don’t match the reality, and the lack of transparency combined with poor and unprofessional service makes for a frustrating and unreliable experience. I would urge anyone considering their Plus membership to be wary—what is advertised does not reflect what you actually receive

My experience with Sesame. My experience with Sesame, particularly regarding prescription issues involving the Walgreens pharmacy on Elliot Road in Tempe, has been frustrating. 

Before last month, I was looking into joining the weight loss program through Sesame. I completed the weight loss questionnaire, after which I was prompted to enter payment information and subscribe to Sesame Plus for $10 per month, which I believed was required in order to participate in the program. However, when I met with the provider, I was told that they were not associated with the weight loss program and that a refund would be issued. I then tried booking another appointment, but I had to call Sesame to confirm that this appointment also was not part of the weight loss program. That appointment had to be canceled, and the agent I spoke with directed me to the correct subscription, called Success by Sesame. Only after enrolling in the proper program was I able to be seen for a Wegovy prescription.

Last month, I also had problems with my prescription. It was initially sent to Walmart Pharmacy, but Walmart told me they had never received the prescription. Because the issue could not be resolved, the prescription eventually had to be transferred to Walgreens. I still had to follow up multiple times and go back and forth trying to determine whether it had actually been sent and what the out-of-pocket cost would be. It took a significant amount of time and effort just to ensure I would be able to get my medication.

This month, my prescription was initially sent to Walgreens. I was unable to see the price on the Sesame website, unlike the first month, (at which time my first prescription also came with a GoodRX coupon by default without going to the manufacturer's website to redeem the manufacturer reduced price, which wasn't the case this time), so I called Walgreens and was told that my out-of-pocket cost would be over $1,000. I declined and had the prescription transferred to CVS instead.

I then went to the manufacturer's website and obtained a coupon that reduced my out-of-pocket cost to $200. After CVS began giving me problems, I decided to transfer the prescription back to Walgreens. Before doing so, I called Walgreens to confirm whether they would accept the coupon, and they assured me that it would bring the cost down to $200. However, Walgreens later told me they never received the prescription after it had been sent from CVS. Even after the prescription was resent to Walgreens, I was told the same thing, and the issue remained unresolved. I then had to contact Sesame again to see what was going on. Sesame told me they had contacted the pharmacy and that I could pick it up at 3 p.m. on 6/17/26. I wanted to call to check on the status with the pharmacy, but I was on hold for a while. After finally getting through, they confirmed that they had received the prescription, that my out-of-pocket cost would be $200, and when it would be ready. Only then was the issue finally resolved.

As a result, I spent hours trying to locate my medication and coordinate between the pharmacies and my provider. I expected clearer communication and better coordination from Sesame. Instead, I experienced confusion, inconsistent information, numerous phone calls, and unnecessary stress.

I am tired of having to jump through hoops to get basic issues resolved, which is why I canceled Sesame and will not be using their service again. The lack of coordination and support throughout this process only added to the frustration

I booked a mental health appointment…. I booked a mental health appointment for anxiety with a specific provider and waited nearly a week for the visit. This wasted my time, affected my health, and reflects poor accountability

Lack of manners, bad doctors. Most doctors there have no interest in you or manners. They just want to prescribe something and get rid of you ASAP. Conversations rarely last more than 5 minutes. It's not even about time spent on me. It's about attitude and lack of common courtesy. They talk to you like you're some fly annoying them and distracting from something important. Looks like they take appointments in the middle of their regular work day and they only care about taking your $50 for 3 minutes of talking to them and getting rid of you ASAP. Often they don't even say bye or disappear if you're 1 minute late.  On top of that, once I was prescribed a horse dosage of medicine that made me sicker than I already was. Simple Google search confirmed that this dose is dangerous. Last time I used it I had bacterial lungs infection, the doctor prescribed me HORMONAL IMMUNE SUPPRESSANT AND CAUGH SUPPRESSANT. Simple Google search and common sense tell anyone with an IQ over 70, that when your immune system is trying to fight a bacteria you shouldn't be trying to suppress it. Caugh helps mucus come out of the lungs. If I stop coughing, it will stay there. Which is why last time I had the same infection a month ago and I was prescribed same meds, it did not help the way it should and the infection reappeared soon again. I will not use Sesame again. $50 for 3 minutes of talking to a bad doctor

Check your credit card statement.  Sesame may be charging you for something you don't expect.. I was using Sesame Care for my GLP prescription.  A year or so ago, I prepaid for a year's supply of GLP medication.  I purchased it that way because the company offered me the opportunity to do so, as they were aware that the prescription would no longer be available within the next year.

10 months later, the prescription was no longer available because the government, in its infinite wisdom, decided to deprive people of the opportunity to buy affordable weight loss medication.  To Sesame's credit, they did give me a refund for the two months of medication that I had paid for but did not receive.  However, when I checked my credit card bill this month, I discovered that Sesame was billing me $89 a month for a Sesame subscription.  I had no idea they would do that (they did not inform me in advance).  The only plan I ever had with Sesame was for weight loss medication, and when THEY (not me) canceled that plan, I assumed that I would owe them no more money.  I was wrong.  Instead, they were charging me $89 a month for a subscription I did not need and did not request.  They would not refund the money I paid for that.  

If you are in the same situation as me, please check your credit card bill carefully.  You, too, might be being charged for something you did not request and do not want

Two stars is generous. Two stars is generous. My experience with Sesame Care has been a failure from start to finish.

The provider I saw was dismissive and clearly trying to rush through the appointment as fast as possible. When I asked him to fix it, he told me he couldn't and that I'd need to contact support.

Support took over a day to respond. When they finally did, they told me the provider had to make the change — the same provider who had just told me he couldn't. It then took several more days of back-and-forth to get a corrected lab order. That's a week of my time wasted on something the provider should have handled correctly the first time, or fixed in five minutes when I flagged it.

To top it off, the booking flow is now broken and I can't even schedule a follow-up. So I've paid for a visit, gotten nothing useful out of it, lost a week chasing a basic correction, and now can't access the platform to move forward.

If you're considering Sesame Care, look elsewhere. The provider quality, the support response time, and the platform itself have all failed. I would not recommend this service to a

I told them my aunt was still in pain and if they couldn't get there within a reasonable amount of time, they should have said that in the beginning, then I would have insisted she go to the ER.  The facility staff also again called for ETA to no avail and was told they were on the other side of town 40 plus mins away doing x-rays there.  Finally that evening, approx. 36 hrs or more later, they came and did the x-ray, but only because we followed up and complained about the lack of triage, lack of protocol for assessing patients and especially because of lack of staff.  I will not use the x-ray portion of DispatchHealth unless it's not urgent or the patient is not in pain.
