Terrible Customers Support, Late Deliveries. These people should be shut down. Neither the app nor the website work. Expect me to wait all day for my antibiotic and Don’t answer their chats, support opens late and cannot get their prescriptions out for 8 hours!

I usually do not leave complaints on…. I usually do not leave complaints on public forums, but I feel compelled to warn others about serious problems with this pharmacy that may effect your health. If you are on maintenance medication, this pharmacy will not consistently honor your prescriptions or reliably make deliveries in a timely manner. This can leave the customer impaired or result in hospitalization. In my opinion, the worst part is their outstandingly polite customer service. They have been so sweet on the phone every single month when there are week long delays (among a myriad other problems like incorrect prescription information on their website, deliveries they claim to have made but never did). Their packaging advertisement even states "we care" and "hello dear" - this comes off as an obvious marketing ploy if you are actively hurting people. They will admit to their error, but then tell your insurance company you lost your pills so they'll cover a new batch or blame your doctor for not providing the right information. I'm a customer of more than three years, so the first few times I suffered through it and moved on, but now I'm on the phone with them every single month since November 2022. It may be convenient to receive your medication by delivery and schedule online, but at what cost? I've sadly decided to leave - their amazing reps and marketing has kept me for long enough

Extremely disappointed. I have been a client of Alto Pharmacy for approximately three years. I have referred several individuals to Alto Pharmacy, and I hold them in high regard, having left numerous positive reviews. I manage multiple medications, and there has not been a single issue that they have been unable to resolve. Their messaging system is exceptionally user-friendly, and their response times are commendably swift. I hold this pharmacy in high esteem.

However, I received a notification yesterday indicating that they would cease supplying my Schedule II medication, which they have provided for nearly two years. The notice did not include an explanation for this discontinuation, and as a 72-year-old individual, I particularly valued the convenience of their medication delivery service, as I prefer to avoid unnecessary travel. I am quite distressed by their decision to discontinue this particular medication, necessitating my search for an alternative pharmacy. I prefer to consolidate all my medications in one location.

At this juncture, I am contemplating whether to continue my relationship with Alto Pharmacy, given that they did not provide any rationale for the cessation of this medication. The absence of an explanation in their correspondence is concerning. This experience is unprecedented for me, as I have never encountered a pharmacy that selectively determines which medications to fill. I am deeply disappointed in their service

Inept and Dangerous. I have been an Alto customer nearly since the inception of the business. I was an early adopter in San Francisco. Unfortunately, the customer support has been on a nosedive, particularly over the last year, and I’ve reached the end of my rope.

If you miss your delivery, they will give your medication to another customer and then tell you it’s out of stock. There’s no way to directly contact your delivery driver, so you have to go through support, but the chat takes hours to respond and the hold time for calls is typically about an hour. 

It seems like Alto has lost sight of the fact that people rely on their medications, and in some cases, it could be a matter of life and death. I have lost countless hours dealing with issues and attempting to contact support. I hate to go through the hassle of changing pharmacies, but unfortunately, Alto has left me no choice. I just can’t trust them anymore and I can’t afford to have my life throw into disarray over and over due to their negligence. 

If you are reading this and have life altering prescriptions (heart medication, insulin, scheduled meds, etc), I am urging you, please look elsewhere for RX delivery. Do not put yourself at risk. Alto may be fine for routine, non-urgent prescriptions, but if you need reliable service, do not use

You said: athenahealth portal is garbageYou are far from alone in this. Widespread user reviews—from patients and clinical staff alike—frequently criticize the Athena Healthcare, Inc. Reviews on Trustpilot and the ATHENAHEALTH Reviews on Yelp for glitchy interfaces, broken login loops, and frustrating automated support.Navigating the platform's clunky design and technical limitations can be incredibly frustrating. Consider these practical workarounds to manage your healthcare needs and bypass the portal:Bypass Login Loops: If the web portal is freezing or giving you an "email not in database" error, try clearing your browser's cache, use an incognito window, or use a different web browser like Chrome, Edge, or Firefox. (A comprehensive list of supported web browsers is detailed in the Patient Portal Frequently Asked Questions - Athenahealth).Contact the Front Desk Directly: Because AthenaHealth is an enterprise software provider, their direct support is geared toward clinic administrators rather than patients. If you are locked out of your medical records or need an urgent prescription refill, call your local doctor's office directly and ask them to manually push a document or reset your registration link on their end.Avoid the Online Check-In Forms: Many users report receiving automated text/email links from the system that glitch when trying to submit paperwork online. If the system is unresponsive, you can usually just ignore the digital link and ask to fill out the paperwork in the waiting room when you arrive

A patient's pov. From a patient pov:  
I pay my bills promptly via online banking thru a major bank, and 99% of the time the provider gets a paper check w/in 5 business days. I have two providers who started using Athena. I would get constant (like daily) email a/o text nags for payment that looked like they came from the provider for any new charge but were sent by Athena.  At first I responded to nags by emailing back saying, "Paid!" w/bank payment record attached - but the "Payment due!!" nags kept on coming. I tried opening an Athena account to stop the nags but found Athena's software so dysfunctional and its privacy policy so mercenary, I returned to online banking bill pay.  
It took several complaints directly to providers to make the nags stop.
Since the dunning emails appear to come directly from the provider, Athena's obnoxious behavior makes it look like providers assume their patients are deadbeats - not a good look, esp. for psychological services. 
Athena's obnoxious, aggressive billing tactics reflect badly on providers already tarred by a predatory, broken healthcare system. Providers: You can do better

Transparency & Truthfulness Matters!. Transparency & Truthfulness Matters!
Especially if you are trying to procure new clients for a high-priced system. 
I approached Athenahealth EHR for a Demo, following which I received a call from a company intake person that took all the info re: our practice, our requirements etc. over almost 30 min on the phone. I was led to believe that the next email I would get would be to schedule the demo.
Following the initial detailed intake phone call, I received an email asking me to schedule another meeting with "senior sales analyst" to "Discuss your practice – what’s going well, where there are challenges, what goals you have for the practice/Review athena’s unique partnership model/Discuss and establish next steps" 

I courteously declined the meeting in reply to email courteously and very clearly explaining the reason for my refusal and my expectation: I had already discussed my needs and my practice specifics and so it would simply be a waste of my time providing the same information all over again not to mention  duplication of information and requested a live demo instead where I could actually see the functionality of the system. This is the industry standard for most EHR companies I have contacted------an initial intake phone followed by a demo setup. 

2 days after I declined the follow-up phone call I get a call in the morning, in the middle of a patient session from their company representative assuring me that they could schedule a demo meeting that afternoon along with the pricing modules made available at the same time if I was agreeable. I scheduled the meeting based on this reassurance.

Come the time for the meeting, first off, the salesperson tells me that he's "not able to hear me!!!", in spite of the system and browser showing repeatedly that the microphone was working just fine." Let's just a few things discuss over the phone and then I can show you the practical side of it on the desktop. We probably won't need the voice function so much for the demo!!!", he says. And guess what? The guy just had me elaborate and repeat all the intake info over the next 45 minutes. THERE WAS NO DEMO. THERE WAS NO DISCUSSION/EXPLANATION OF ANY PRICE MODULES. And when I asked re: moving on to the demo, here is his answer: "Oh, looks like we have already used up almost an hour, so there's really no time left for the demo!! And any way this was a meeting more to know about your practice. I'm going to email you some demo videos so you can review them at your convenience!!"

Really? You wasted an hour of my time, lying to me and leading me on to attend this "DEMO" meeting, just to tell me that you were chatting with me and that I could review your emailed "demo videos?" (half of which I probably wouldn't even understand without an in-person explanation of the different functionalities). No wonder, you couldn't "hear me." 
And then to top it off, he tells me that I would need to share my practice financials in order for them to give me "the most accurate, practice specific quote", and "we can definitely schedule another meeting to go over that". Seriously???!!!

So, NO DEMO & NO PRICING MODULES!!!! Just lies and waste of time. 

So, AthenaHealth, let me tell you how to impress and procure new clients: You impress and procure new clients by NOT LYING TO THEM, by respecting their time constraints, by honoring your word and by NOT LEADING THEM ON.  You do not get new clients by the shabby and deplorable behavior your employees demonstrated. This is the worst customer service that I've had the misfortune to come across. Lying and tricking your prospective clients may work in the short run to get them in to your "meetings" but that is no way to create a good impression. Your company may be "the most used EHR company" but it is the worst in customer service. 

I would neither recommend you nor your services to any of my colleagues. 

Extremely, extremely angry and never-to-be-a-client, 
Rita U. Goradia, MD

Catastrophic Experience With @athenahealth – Small Practices Beware. I signed with @athenahealth in July 2024 after months of demos and referrals. We went live November 19th. Since then, my clinic has endured one of the most disruptive, financially damaging, and exhausting experiences of my career.

Claim Failures:

No claims sent for the first 6+ weeks due to providers not configured to sign notes.

Lab billing delayed more than 10 weeks; even now Athena forces a modifier that causes denials.

DME billing delayed 16+ weeks; workflow still broken.

Prior authorizations not stored — every claim must be corrected manually.

Chiropractic Medicare claims require manual entry of initial treatment date on every visit.

Even today, 5 payers’ claims still cannot be sent.


False Promises:

Sales claimed all lab integrations → only LabCorp, and delayed.

Claimed chiropractic macros → none exist.

Claimed care plan countdowns → don’t exist.

Claimed supplement/product sales with tax → only possible by billing fake insurance claims ($6–$7 fee each).


Patient Harm:

Patients charged random amounts due to faulty care plan setups.

Incorrect statements mailed, emailed, and texted.

Lost patients due to destroyed trust.

Others constantly complain about redundant reminders.


Operational & Financial Chaos:

Fee schedule uploaded 3 times, leaving erroneous codes.

Sales tax calculated by hand.

Nearly 100 support cases opened, with hours on calls and little resolution.

Timely filing denials piling up because Athena delays triggered deadlines before claims were even sent.

$15,000 paid for an AI workaround just to survive.

Athena now demands $1,000–$3,000/month for API access to my own data.


Recordings: Athena refuses to release sales/onboarding recordings they promised were kept “for quality purposes,” likely to avoid proof of misrepresentation.

👉 Bottom line: If you are a small or independent practice, do not sign with @athenahealth. The cost — financial, operational, and emotional — is catastrophic.

#Healthcare #MedicalPractice #IndependentPractice #EMR #Athenahealth #HealthIT #PracticeManagement #PatientCare

I'm a business owner and opted to use Athenahealth's services based on attractiveness of the clinical platform and access to HIE within the EMR. I figured that we have a biller who does our billing and no EMR/PM system could mess that up.

Man, was I wrong. Athenahealth managed to be both absurdly incompetent and paternalistic toward our practice, setting up unnecessary and time-consuming requirements that only prevented us from billing and getting paid. Case in point: our biller resubmitted claims for a major payer SEVERAL times. We were nine months behind on getting actual $ from this payer. Athenahealth kept blocking the claims for pointless reasons. Finally, we broke our contract and submitted the claims through Office Ally. The claims were accepted on the first try. This is just one example of the miserable incompetence that defines Athenahealth.

We switched EMR/PM systems after about 11 months with Athenahealth, and of course it was a huge hassle for everyone including the providers, but it was worth it, and we're in better shape now. I hope this review can help someone avoid this type of experience. 

I recommend avoiding this company at all costs, especially if you are a multistate practice. It is truly a billing nightmare. Their clinical platform is fairly nice, but not worth the rest of the insulting and incompetent treatment

No transparency, refusal to use email for communications, and critical information left out. Our experience with Tebra has been extremely disappointing and has negatively impacted the launch of our new practice.

During the sales process, we were encouraged to move forward with what was presented as an opportunity to "try out" the platform. What was not clearly disclosed was that signing the agreement effectively committed us to paying for an entire year, regardless of whether the platform met our needs. Had this been presented more transparently, we would have made a different decision.

Since realizing we were contractually obligated, we invested significant time completing Tebra University trainings. Despite completing the available education, we still found the platform difficult to navigate, and many of the questions critical to our practice operations were not addressed in the training materials.

We repeatedly requested information via email regarding onboarding requirements and billing setup. Instead, we were continuously told that the information could only be provided during an onboarding call. Scheduling those calls proved difficult because available appointment times rarely aligned with our schedules. Requests for email communication were repeatedly declined.

When we finally completed the onboarding session—more than two months after our initial demo—we were informed that additional steps were required before we could bill Medicare and Medicaid and that there would be an approximate 35-day delay even after credentialing. This information was essential to our business planning and could have been communicated in a simple email on day one. Had it been disclosed earlier, we could have begun those processes immediately rather than losing valuable time and revenue.

Perhaps most frustrating was the lack of accountability. At no point did anyone acknowledge how these delays affected our practice or offer meaningful solutions. Instead, the responsibility was largely placed on us for not being able to attend onboarding meetings that could have been replaced with basic written communication.

We have also received invoices that do not appear to align with the pricing discussed during the sales process, creating additional concerns regarding transparency.

For a company that markets itself as a solution for healthcare practices, the overall experience has felt unnecessarily complicated, poorly communicated, and lacking in professionalism. The delays and lack of transparency have cost our startup valuable time and income during a critical growth period.

Based on our experience, I would not recommend Tebra to new practices seeking a straightforward, transparent, and supportive EHR and billing solution. There are alternative platforms that, in our opinion, provide a more user-friendly experience and clearer communication

Reposting after Trustpilot removed my…. Reposting after Trustpilot removed my legitimate review under pressure from Tebra. I have the emails & reciepts!  

I ran a cash-based practice on Tebra, formerly Kareo, for nearly five years. The sales process is where they shine. The demos are polished, the promises are impressive, and none of it reflects the reality of daily use.
Patient appointment notifications were unreliable at best and completely absent at worst. I submitted support cases asking why patients were not receiving confirmations. Those cases sat unanswered for months. My patients missed appointments because the system failed to communicate with them and no one at Tebra could explain or fix it.
When I needed support for an actual clinical workflow issue, there was no one. Customer service for anything touching real practice operations does not exist. Cases opened sat for months without resolutions.
The telehealth feature was not a clinical tool. It was a checkbox on a marketing page.
When I cancelled and moved to a competitor at one-third the cost, Tebra billed me for an additional month citing a 30-day notice requirement that did not exist in my original Kareo contract. That is not a policy. That is THEFT.
After I left and posted my honest experience, Tebra had the audacity to contact me asking me to remove the review. When I refused, they went to Trustpilot. Trustpilot emailed me. I responded confirming this was a legitimate review based on nearly five years of documented experience. They removed it anyway.
That sequence of events raises a serious question about what Trustpilot actually is. If a platform emails a reviewer asking them to confirm authenticity, receives confirmation, and still removes the review after a paying advertiser applies pressure, they are not a review platform. They are a reputation management service for companies that can afford to use them.

I cannot name a single strength of Tebra. I have receipts & email communications for everything I have stated here. Providers deserve better. There are better systems. Find one

Zero stars- Avoid!. zero stars really. And Trust pilot already removed my first review! Let that sink in. A company so committed to manufactured credibility that they scrub legitimate complaints from a clinician with five years of documented experience. 

I have been a Tebra customer since before it was Tebra, when it was still Kareo. Five years of premium payments for a system that routinely failed at its most basic functions. Appointment notifications that did not work despite correct setup and repeated escalations. Forms that failed to submit. Claims that did not process. Billing issues that sat unresolved for weeks because their customer service model is built on attrition. You call, they tell you to email. You email, you hear nothing. You call again, you get someone new who cannot help you and starts the cycle over. These are clinical and financial issues. A week of silence from a vendor managing your scheduling and billing is not acceptable in any industry, and it is dangerous in healthcare.
On top of the dysfunction, they nickel and dime you for every additional feature while refusing to be accountable for the core services you are already overpaying for.
I have the invoices. I have the support tickets. I have the communication records. Every word of this is documented.
If Trustpilot removes this review, they are telling you exactly who they work for, and it is not the consumer. Tebra pays to look reputable. Do not be fooled by their rating on this platform.I do not leave reviews lightly. Run.

I rarely leave negative reviews. I rarely leave negative reviews, but this EHR system has been one of the most frustrating platforms I have ever worked with in healthcare administration. What should streamline workflows instead creates constant inefficiencies, billing delays, and unnecessary staff burden.

The user interface is outdated and incredibly unintuitive. Simple tasks require multiple unnecessary steps, pages freeze regularly, and reporting functionality is extremely limited unless you manually manipulate data outside the system. For a program that is supposed to support healthcare operations, it consistently slows them down.

The billing side is especially problematic. Claims management lacks transparency, payment posting workflows are clunky, and troubleshooting issues often feels impossible because error messaging is vague or nonexistent. Staff spend more time trying to work around the software than actually completing productive work.

Customer support has also been disappointing. Response times are slow, issues frequently go unresolved, and the answers provided often feel scripted rather than genuinely helpful. Critical problems impacting operations should not take days or weeks to address.

For the amount organizations invest in EHR systems, there should be a significantly higher standard for functionality, reliability, and support. Unfortunately, this platform has created more operational stress than efficiency for our team

I have been a Tebra (formerly Kareo) customer for 9 years and used their platform as the EMR and billing system for my physical therapy clinic in Phoenix, AZ.

In November 2025, I notified Tebra that our clinic would be transitioning to a cash-pay model starting January 1, 2026. Because insurance claims take time to finalize, we arranged with their team to downgrade our services for several months so we could close out remaining claims before canceling the platform completely. This downgrade and the new monthly rate were confirmed with their team.

However, beginning in January 2026, Tebra started charging thousands of dollars above the agreed rate for services we were no longer using.

After attempting to resolve the issue and disputing one incorrect charge, Tebra suspended our account overnight and locked us out of our entire system without warning or communication.

This system contains patient records and billing data that are critical to operating a healthcare practice, and the sudden lockout caused significant disruption to our business.

After nearly a decade as a loyal customer, the lack of communication and accountability has been extremely disappointing. Healthcare practices rely heavily on these systems, and sudden account suspension during a billing dispute is incredibly concerning.

I strongly encourage other healthcare practice owners to carefully review billing practices and service agreements before relying on this platform

5 Years of Frustration – Avoid at All Costs. If I could rate below zero, I would.
After five years with Tebra (formerly Kareo), I have not a single positive thing to report. I stayed far too long hoping things would improve. They never did. This was a cash-only business, no insurance claims, and I still could not make this platform work. I eventually switched both practices and never looked back.
1. Customer Support is Non-Existent:
The most glaring issue is the complete failure of their support team. You submit a ticket, they promise a response in 24 to 48 hours, and then nothing. Days pass. Weeks pass. In some cases, months go by with zero resolution. For a clinical platform where timely support is critical to patient care and practice operations, this is completely unacceptable.
2. Clinical Functionality is Outdated and Overpriced
The charting system is clunky, outdated, and not worth what they charge. For a premium price, you would expect a modern, intuitive EHR. What you get is the opposite. To make matters worse, they charge extra for limited functionality that competitors include as standard.
3. Communication Failures That Impact Patient Care:
Appointment reminders and patient messages either fail to send or arrive significantly delayed. The online profile and booking capabilities were so unreliable we had to switch back to Square for scheduling. In a medical practice, unreliable communication is not a minor inconvenience; it directly impacts patient care and no-show rates.
4. Predatory Billing Practices:
When I requested cancellation, they forced me to pay for an additional 30 days, claiming it was in my contract. When I asked to see the contract I had signed, they could not produce one, because I never signed one. I was exhausted and just wanted to be done with them. They still charged me $184 I did not owe.

After five years, I moved my practice to a platform that is cheaper, more functional, and actually supported. The difference was immediate and dramatic.
If you are considering Tebra, please learn from my experience. There are far better and more affordable EMR options available. Tebra needs to be held accountable for what it puts healthcare providers through

I've been paying for this platform for…. I've been paying for this platform for at least a year now (I signed up sometime between August and October 2024), and I still can’t use it as intended. The onboarding process was decent, but several key promises were never fulfilled. I chose this platform specifically because it was marketed as an all-in-one solution—including billing, EHR, e-prescribing, and telehealth.

I’ve been working through the credentialing process with all major insurance carriers, and I was told that once I input the insurance information into Tebra, the Trizetto Clearinghouse would be automatically connected. That never happened.

Since then, I’ve made more than 50 calls—to Trizetto, Medicaid, Medicare, Blue Cross Blue Shield, Aetna, Humana, and others—because the PDF instructions were unclear, incomplete, and in some cases, even the insurance companies themselves didn’t understand them. Trizetto refused to help, explaining that Tebra doesn’t pay for technical support access for their customers. When I contacted Tebra, the representative admitted she was unfamiliar with Trizetto’s system and that this integration was new to her.

Tebra’s support team has been unable to assist, and during a critical time, my onboarding contact was out of office for two weeks. Even after she returned, no resolution was provided.

As it stands now, I have an EHR I’ve paid for but not been able to use for nearly a year. I recently received an email notifying me of a 3% price increase starting in August, despite the billing function still not being connected. I was originally told my contract would end in August, but when I called this week, I was informed it actually ends in October. I’ve been paying \$178/month for a service that’s never functioned as promised.

When I requested to cancel, I was told a support ticket had to be submitted, and that I’d have to wait to hear from the “Loyalty Team.” On July 16, 2025, I finally received a response—just a calendar invite to discuss cancellation.

At this point, I strongly urge others: **Run, don’t walk away from this company.** The promises don’t match the reality, and the lack of accountability has been incredibly frustrating

Our practice has been a client of Tebra (previously Patientpop) since 2019. Our practice has been a client of Tebra (previously Patientpop ) since 2019.  We were initially very happy with the service when the company was much smaller and more focused on the individual clients.  Unfortunately, as the company has grown and ultimately merged with Kareo and renamed Tebra, customer support and our satisfaction with it, has steadily declined.  We currently use their services for our website, scheduling and on-line reputation management through their surveys.  Most recently, we had a terrible incident with Tebra and our reputation management.  They carelessly sent another practice’s patients (147 in total ) our patient survey.  As expected, these patients were not happy to receive another practice’s survey and several responded negatively, with one patient responding extremely aggressively and ultimately began threatening our practice if we did not stop sending the surveys.  We notified Tebra’s implementation team immediately of their error, asking them to rectify the situation and stop sending our surveys to this other practice’s patients.  We even shared the hostile messages that were received.  The implementation team NEVER acknowledged or responded to our emails.  Sharing or co-mingling patient information with another unaffiliated practice is not only extremely negligent, it also risks violating existing HIPAA regulations and the Associate agreement they signed with us.  Their complete lack of concern over any of these factors is both alarming and worrisome.  Needless to say, we regret having continued our relationship with them over the years and would encourage any potential customers to think long and hard before moving forward with this company

I have been using Tebra (formerly…kareo. I have been using Tebra (formerly Kareo) for years and have spent over $80,000 on their services, yet I still struggle with basic reporting and software functionality. One of the most critical reports—showing a patient’s name, date of birth, insurance ID, date of service, amount charged, amount expected, and amount paid—does not exist in a single, easy-to-generate format. Instead, they require users to download multiple reports and manually piece the data together. When I asked for a solution, they told me I could "request a future feature" but offered no immediate fix. Patient statements also fail to show the total amount due—only what the patient owes—leaving patients unaware of their actual financial responsibility. This could be resolved by displaying amounts currently under insurance processing alongside processed charges at the time the statement is sent. These may seem like small details, but they are critical for both patient clarity and efficient practice management.
Beyond reporting issues, Tebra is plagued with frequent outages and software glitches. Our office has been unable to process clinical documentation, billing, and scheduling for two to three days at a time due to system failures. Their only response? A small credit that doesn’t begin to compensate for the disruption.mOther glitches are just as frustrating. For example, an appointment scheduled at 10 AM might simultaneously appear under cancellations or another column, causing double bookings or scheduling conflicts. There is also no proper documentation of patient check-in and check-out times, which is essential for Medicare compliance. When an insurance company questioned whether a patient was present for their full therapy session, I had no way to prove it because Tebra does not track this information correctly. Once again, they told me this was a "future feature request".I write this review in the hope that Tebra will finally address these long-standing issues. Unfortunately, after years of feedback, they are still not listening to their customers. If you are considering Tebra, be warned—you may find yourself stuck in an expensive, inefficient system that fails to meet basic operational needs. Either fix your software or be honest with your clients

Updated: BEWARE: Tebra/Kareo is a Scam of an EMR. BEWARE: Tebra/Kareo is a Scam of an EMR
I recently opened a small, single-provider clinic, and Tebra seemed like an affordable option—but the reality is far from what they promise when they sell you the program.

--Training is Rushed & Inflexible!--
The training program is a “wham, bam, thank you ma’am” experience. You cannot adjust the training timeline—it’s set on their schedule, not yours.
After six weeks, you’re kicked out of their initial training, and your main resource becomes Tebra University, which is terrible. It has no AI capability and reminds me of the old Microsoft Word’s Help menu with the paperclip guy from the 90s, except worse.
--A Skeleton EMR With Clunky Functionality--
Once you complete training, you’re left paying for an EMR skeleton:
-The charting menus are a box-checking nightmare with limited options (e.g., you can’t even select “irregularly irregular” heart rhythm).

-If you’re a private-pay clinic but want to generate CMS-1500 forms, Tebra does not provide a direct way to do this.

-Want to send customized patient forms? Hope you’re a programmer! Their form creation system is clunky and unintuitive.

-The new patient paperwork patients fill out online does not integrate into the EMR—so you or your staff must waste time manually entering it.

-You can upload a maximum of five customizable forms, but patients must print them out, fill them out, and return them to the clinic. Then, you or your staff get the privilege of scanning everything into the EMR manually—because, apparently, your time isn’t valuable.

--Basic Features Are Missing or Useless--
-No built-in work or school notes—you have to create your own from scratch.

-No way to send emails with attachments—their “email” system is just a glorified messaging feature that doesn’t allow attachments. But don’t worry—you can use emojis! Because that’s what matters most in a medical practice, right?

-The payment processing system—I didn’t use it, but if it’s anything like the rest of this company, good luck getting paid!

--Tricky Contracts & Hidden Fees--
-If you qualify for a “60-day free trial,” it’s actually tacked onto the start of your contract, meaning you’re locked in for 14 months instead of 12. So you do pay for the 60 days in the end if you decide to cancel early.

-After six months, once you get your bearings and realize you’ve been royally scammed, you have no reasonable way to cancel or buy out your contract.
Their cancellation fee is your monthly minimum payment multiplied by the remaining months on your contract—so you get a final bomb payment at the time of cancellation just to escape!

--Final Thoughts--
I deeply regret signing up for this EMR. As a new clinic owner, I was learning the operational side of a medical practice, and this was a hard lesson in how deceptive some companies can be. If this review saves even one person from making the mistake of signing up for Tebra/Kareo, I’ll consider it my refund. Learn from my headache and heartache—DO NOT SIGN UP FOR THIS PRODUCT.

Deceptive Sales Practices – PROCEED WITH CAUTION!. I initially had a frustrating experience with Tebra due to misleading sales tactics. The sales rep misrepresented key system features that were critical to my practice, leading me to sign up under false pretenses. Once I realized the system lacked the promised functionality, I never even used it.

At first, my concerns were ignored, and I was threatened with early termination fees, late fees, and collections for a service I never used. I filed a formal dispute, providing proof of the misrepresentation, and submitted complaints to the FTC, Better Business Bureau, and California Attorney General.

After significant effort on my part, Tebra has now agreed to waive the early termination fee, which I appreciate. However, it should not have taken this level of persistence to resolve a legitimate dispute.

Looking at their 1.3-star rating on Trustpilot with 368+ reviews, my experience is far from unique. Many customers report:
🚨 Sales reps misrepresenting system capabilities
🚨 Unfair contracts that are difficult to exit
🚨 Poor customer support when issues arise
🚨 Threats of collections instead of genuine dispute resolution

If you are considering Tebra, I strongly encourage you to do thorough research before signing anything. While they ultimately resolved my case, the process was unnecessarily stressful, and I believe businesses should be held accountable for how they treat their customers

Great product but difficult and inefficient customer care. I would give Tebra five stars for the platform itself—the product is strong overall. That said, the practice management side is fairly clunky and could use improvement, so I would rate the product a 4 out of 5.

The process is highly redundant, time-consuming, and often lacks efficient resolution. There have also been instances where unauthorized changes were made to my account by customer care representatives, which then required additional time on my end to identify and fix. I’ve also had to correct multiple financial-related issues that originated on the support side.

A Warning to Practices: DrChrono’s "Exit Traps" and Support Barriers. If you are considering DrChrono, you need to be aware of what happens when you decide to leave—because they make the process intentionally difficult, expensive, and technically exhausting.

1. Predatory Terms and Billing
-Unilateral Term Changes: They change their online terms without notification. Be warned: simply paying your regular bill constitutes "agreement" to whatever new terms they’ve posted.

-Cancellation Penalties: If you stop payment due to dissatisfaction, they consider it "damages" and will attempt to bill you for the entire remainder of your contract term.

2. The Support Gauntlet
-Phone Support is Non-Existent: Their IVR system is programmed to hang up on you if you’ve been waiting for ~5 minutes. It does not save your place in line; it simply terminates the call.

-Gatekeeping Account Managers: Only an Account Manager can cancel a contract, but you cannot be transferred to one. You must use the Chat tool, which uses "Urgent" as a de-escalation word that carries no actual weight for follow-up.

-Canned Responses: Account Managers appear to have "solve goals" rather than "help goals." Expect generic, canned responses even when you provide detailed evidence of issues. We were flatly told, "You're not eligible to leave our service," despite our documented grievances.

3. The Data Export Nightmare
-When you finally manage to leave, don't expect a clean transition.

-The "survey": Your data request must go through a Typeform survey sent to an Account Manager. There is no ticket number or confirmation—it simply goes into a database with no way to track the approval status.

-Data Fragmentation: You won't receive a cohesive database. You will receive hundreds of individual CSV files (we received over 100 for demographics alone).

-Manual Reconstruction Required: To get your patient records back, you will have to manually map "Client IDs" to "Appointment IDs" across multiple files to piece visit notes back together. It is a massive technical burden for any practice.

Bottom Line: DrChrono may work for you initially, but they have built a system designed to hold your data and your bank account hostage the moment you are no longer satisfied. Proceed with extreme caution

Paper Charts Would Be an Upgrade. It was great when we first started using it in 2018. Since EverHealth took over, it has steadily declined to the point where it’s difficult to recognize it as the same product.

The new interface is a major step backward. It’s cluttered, unintuitive, and clearly not designed with real clinical workflows in mind. Tasks that used to be quick and efficient now take significantly longer due to unnecessary steps and poor navigation. Instead of supporting the user, it consistently gets in the way.

Performance has also deteriorated. The system is slow, laggy, and often unresponsive at the worst possible times. In a clinical setting, where efficiency and reliability are critical, this creates constant friction throughout the day.

Customer support is another major issue. When problems arise, responses are delayed or unhelpful, and there’s little sense of urgency or ownership. It feels like support has become an afterthought rather than a core part of the service.

What’s most disappointing is that this platform used to work well. The current version feels overcomplicated, under-supported, and increasingly out of touch with the needs of its users.

At this point, even going back to paper charts would feel like an upgrade in terms of reliability and efficiency—which is not something anyone expects to say about modern software.

If you’re evaluating EHR options, I would strongly recommend looking elsewhere

Very poor exprience. Our organization transitioned to DrChrono expecting a stable, enterprise-level EHR suitable for a multi-provider medical practice. While reporting functionality exists, it is not intuitive. Data that appears straightforward, such as scheduled patient activity, often requires additional, non-obvious steps to be captured accurately in reports, making routine KPI tracking inefficient.

Billing stability required nearly a year of repeated troubleshooting. Even now, the system can behave inconsistently—clinical entries such as CPT codes or diagnoses may not save properly, disappear, or duplicate, and information that appears correct during documentation may display differently once routed to billing.

Patient statements are also not patient-friendly. They resemble internal accounting ledgers rather than clear, understandable statements, requiring staff to manually recreate statements upon request, adding unnecessary administrative burden.

Support has generally been courteous, but issue resolution has been slow, with timelines frequently shifting. We would also advise practices to carefully review contract terms, as some provisions are non-standard, including continued provider licensing charges even after an associate is no longer with the organization.

Overall, the platform has required significant workarounds and staff time to maintain basic operational clarity. We recommend that practices thoroughly test workflows and review contractual details before committing

We moved to DrChrono expecting an…. We moved to DrChrono expecting an enterprise-level EHR. While reporting technically exists, it is not intuitive or user-friendly. Even when a patient is clearly on the schedule, reports often require extra, non-obvious steps to capture data correctly, making routine KPI tracking inefficient and error-prone.

Billing took nearly a year of back-and-forth to stabilize, and even now the system frequently glitches. CPT codes, diagnoses, and entries may disappear, fail to save, or duplicate. What appears correct on the clinical side can show differently once sent to billing, creating real risk and added cleanup work.

Patient statements are also a major weakness. They read like internal accounting ledgers rather than clear, patient-friendly statements, forcing staff to manually recreate statements each time a patient requests one.

Support is generally polite, but resolution is slow and often reactive. In addition, practices should read the contract very carefully. Some terms are non-standard and unfavorable, such as continuing to pay for provider licenses even after an associate has left the clinic.

Overall, this platform has required far more workarounds and staff time than expected

Billing Dispute and Service Discontinuation Concerns. We used DrChrono as the EHR platform for our medical practice beginning in January 2023 and ultimately decided to part ways due to persistent billing and support issues.

During our time on the platform, we were charged a per-provider fee even after a provider was no longer employed or using the system. We raised this issue multiple times over several months and requested available remedies to align fees with actual provider usage, but were told adjustments could not be made due to renewal timing.

After formally disputing the charges and attempting to resolve the matter directly, our account was referred to collections while the charges remained disputed. Platform access was discontinued during a period when insurance receivables were delayed due to a government shutdown, which created additional operational challenges for a primarily Medicare-based practice.

After nearly two years on the platform, this experience made it difficult to resolve billing concerns in good faith or exit the relationship cleanly. Practices considering DrChrono, particularly those with a high Medicare patient mix, should carefully review contract terms related to provider counts, renewals, access to data, and dispute resolution before committing

Don't be DUPED!. I chose Dr. Chrono because I needed an EHR to get me across the country (a lot less expensive than HIPAA compliant mover of paper charts).  My expectation after the demo was vastly different than what was delivered.  I've used it for four (mostly) excruciating months. 
Some might say - buyer beware - so I'll lay out a few things that were either not true, or just frustrating. 
1) Tele-medicine through Chrono is limited to 80 hours a MONTH.  I have 100% Telehealth practice and they knew that in the demo call. They will charge you an additional fee if you need more hours...significantly more than a Zoom Pro annual subscription. 
2) Onboarding is scripted and when you ask a question, they have trouble moving away from the script. 
3) Patients do not know when they have a message from OnPatient - no text available for this.
4) You will likely have to create your own note templates which are clunky and takes a significant amount of time to learn how to do them.  It took me just under a week and multiple videos that didn't explain what was needed.  So much wasted time doing this - it could have been much more streamlined. 
5) Superbill template cannot be altered and requires a signature which leads to needing another product to complete it.  It was not useful - I made my own. 
6) Online "help" documentation says it's updated, but there is no way they actually look at it.  The images they use are old and don't always related to the verbiage which is incomplete or unclear. There is a "was this article helpful" button at the bottom.  Out of 25-50 articles I read, maybe two had more positives than negatives. 
7) Clicking - clicking - clicking - click - click - click - click - click - clicking - clicking - click - and - click.
8) Feedback - they don't ask... well, at least not until I'd decided to look elsewhere, so I followed the link to the survey.  You have to sign up for a Microsoft account in order to participate.  
9) Stripe is NOT integrated.
10) Bright point was when I used a patient's phone number in "search" and their name actually came up.  That was it.  
Bottom line - Dr. Chrono is clunky, overpriced for what they ACTUALLY deliver and very far from being remotely intuitive. Save yourself the hassle - look elsewhere

AVOID THEM.  SERVICE IS TERRIBLE.. I have been with DrChrono for about 10 years - a "loyal" customer.  At the time we signed on we knew it was a fairly new company in a "new world" of EHR.  The concept is great.  I created many custom templates.  The big problem, as many other reviewers have noted, is HORRIBLE SERVICE and also frequent breakdowns.  I was promised MANY times that I would have ONE contact person.  Not only did this not happen until recently, but on the occasions when someone in the past told me they WERE the contact person, a few days later they were no longer with Chrono, and I was told they do NOT offer a single point of contact for service.  They don't leave phone numbers to call unless you manage to get one from someone answering your emails.  Overall, I find the system easy to use WHEN IT WORKS, but service is so poor I regret wasting all these years trying to get good service.  Also, when they switched to a new cloud service, the up time went the H-E double hockey sticks.  Their service performance site completely misrepresents their uptime (or should I say downtimes).  Even today, the on patient portal was NOT functioning well enough to consent patients in a timely way.  It was NOT listed as having a problem.  We see this time and time again.  There are many other service issues.  I'm glad I never used their billing given what I read above.  AVOID THIS COMPANY.  They do NOT live up to the promises of the product which has great potential.  SERVICE is everything.  I don't feel that their service leadership truly understands what it means to not have good communication, rapid resolution of problems, and true customer satisfaction for a medical practice that is small.  It is highly disruptive when it goes down, and at times you can't even report a problem since doing so requires the system to be up and running!
PS:  I just read the response below.  The response is exactly what I've been told MANY MANY times over the past 10 years regarding this same issue of poor service.  Frankly, I would venture to guess  this is not a PEOPLE problem.  It is a system problem.  You really need to assign a single person (with clearly identified back up when that person is on vacation or not available) as a service point of contact that would include sales/service/etc.  The T-Mobile service model might be applied.  Ergo, the response to this is more of the same and an indication that once again the system is completely failing customers that need service.  And believe me, this is hardly the only "down" time they have, and their charts/graphs of down time don't come close to showing service problems

I have used Dr Chrono for one year. I have used Dr Chrono for one year.  Positives- it has concise notes, that you can alter to your practice, easy navigation.
Negatives- horrible customer service, expect response to your "support ticket" form days to month after. No way to contact anyone by phone. Constant , unannounced change of your account manager and other support  people, assigned to your account. 
I had an "accidental" merge of over 2000 patient to my account from a different practice and it took me close to 1 week, to get someone's attention and have it removed. I am now fighting to stop unauthorized billing for termination of service, for over two months.. It's as messy and disorganized as it can be,,DO not chose!!

Scammed. In early 2019 we were scammed into paying for a year of DrChrono for a little over $2,000. We were left in the cold during implementation and the manager stopped returning our calls. We finally had enough and asked for a refund as it wasn't going to work for us. We were declined our refund and they deactivated our access after we told them we weren't going to use an EHR that wasn't fully up and running for us (nor met our needs). I've since heard this same story for many practices in my area that gave DrChrono a shot. Beware and move on to the next EHR

No improvement with the new owner.. Update 10/2024
They have changed their help ticket format. There is no longer somewhere to view progress on all open tickets.  We had to make a spreadsheet ourselves to track ticket numbers, so we can email them and ask them for status updates. 

There is no real way to export patient files to a new EHR.  8 years of records for 8 providers will be an absolute nightmare. We’re basically hostages.

UPDATE: management did mention that they saw this review and gave me a credit on my statement. They have not, however, solved help tickets that have been open for 6+ weeks. So my assessment of them stands.  Buyer beware. Actually, don’t beware. Choose a different vendor.


We’ve been with dr chrono for almost 5 years.  The first year was really good.  And then things went steadily downhill, and the service is now abysmal.  

They do a decent job at initial onboarding, when you first sign the contract....because they still are trying to impress you.  But later on when a new provider joins your group, be prepared for several months of pulling your hair out as you try to get the new person on boarded.  Your new provider will receive no training.  YOU will have to be on DrChrono about every detail, because they certainly don’t have an onboarding punch list. Keep in mind that if a provider leaves your practice, they will keep charging you for that provider until your annual contract ends.  Even if you replace that provider. 

My newest provider has been waiting a month for them to get his credit card processing working.  This after waiting 1.5 months for them to set up his claim submission correctly. So here we are 3 months and almost $1000 in monthly fees in and he still can’t fully collect payment for his services.

What else?  Automatic appointment reminders may or may not get sent. Your note or that appointment you’re trying to make may or may not get saved.  Electronic statements that never make it to the patient portal.  Claims that give the appearance of having been sent, but never make it to the insurance company... All “customer service” is via help tickets, no live assistance.  They advertise integration with quest and labcorp, but labcorp refuses to integrate unless you’re a huge practice, so don’t count on it. Quest did eventually get integrated with us, but it took months and months and me going a little bit ballistic before it actually got done.

They recently announced that they are moving their servers to AWS, and it will solve all the problems. They’ve been saying some thing or another will fix things for years now

We only started using their system and…. We only started using their system and they have removed patient data, demographics,clinical notes,billing and appointments. The customer service has been non existent. Reading these reviews is making me think that I  am going to look to get out before I get in any deeper. Seems to be a good program. The foundation is there but the  customer service is just not there. Empty promises.  Only work during the week. Ridiculous too hard to reach. They mess up the accounts with no plan to correct. Not professional

Promises...... Many promises about customization but really when it came down to it, they offered to load a few forms but counted one form with two pages as multiple forms.  So many promises but very delayed responses.  I just realized that even on the lowest end this is very pricey for a fraction of features offered elsewhere

DO NOT GO NEAR THIS EMR. DO NOT GO NEAR THIS EMR.

With an incorrect set of patient demographics what do you think I could do with additional months. After genuinely trying to sort this out for months with you,the ship of trust & reliability has sailed

I've been forced to use this awful EMR…. I've been forced to use this awful EMR for over a year.  Do NOT purchase this!  I'm a PA-C and the amount of time I've spent dealing with this glitchy EMR is insane.  After you've completed your superbill I'd say 70% of the time it does not save and you have to reenter the entire thing.  Not fun when you have 3 patients waiting for you! We changed from Practice Fusion to…. We changed from Practice Fusion to Drchrono a year ago and it has been a nightmare. It takes multiple clicks for any action. Patient portal messages are all on separate threads so when one replies the original message is not attached and you have to go back and open all of the patient messages in order to recall the concern and reply appropriately. There are multiple patches for labs and radiology orders/results.  We can order Labcorp and Quest labs through the lab ordering section but if we want to order labs through West Pacific or any other lab we have to go through the  Health Gorilla patch. Lab orders come in through the lab ordering section and it is confusing if the lab order was given in May and the patient had the labs done in August. The results are all over the place. Sometimes they don't come in through the lab order section and are received as documents. The system is very fragmented. The customer service is very poor as it takes days or longer for a response to any concern and while the staff is courteous and interested there is usually no resolution. At one point we were asked for constructive input and spent a couple of hours providing this with no response. There is an option for voice activated dictation for progress notes but this only works for iphones or ipad, not for android. It is bad business to limit this service. The erx is ok but any medication that was ordered more than 6 months ago has to be reordered, cannot be refilled as the original entity has somehow timed out. Click click click.  The favorable parts are that documents can be easily emailed to patients if they are signed up for their portal and efaxing is very easy. Patients like the portal and the system is easy to learn but with that learning comes an awareness of the many shortcomings
We are changing to a new system as of 1/1/20 that will hopefully be better. Perhaps this is the status quo of EHR but hopefully there is a better product out there somewhere. Practice Fusion was a reasonable and economical program but did not meet the needs of our growing practice. Drchrono was clearly a step backwards. Would not recommend this program. If you are solo practice or less than 5 providers it would be worth checking in to Practice Fusion. Otherwise good luck!
Horrible so far. Horrible so far. Do yourself a favor and use another platform. Customer service slow to respond and then they give inaccurate answers that don’t help

This company is the worst I've ever had…. This company is the worst I've ever had to deal with in medical software. Their sales team will tell you literally anything you want to hear in order to complete the sale. They will then aggressively push a one-year payment in advance(usually it's month-to-month), and when you bite they've got you. During the implementation process our team had many concerns, but none of them were getting answered. When we complained on how long it was taking to complete the implementation process and how little the specialist was doing, management got confrontational with us and started ignoring our calls or hanging up on us. We've since been attempting to get either all or some of our money back, but the company is holding on tight and willing to refund. BEWARE!

Most horrendous experience with a company in my career. A DrChrono sales rep convinced my secretary that DrChrono would make the office better and then signed a contract with her (not me the doctor and owner of the practice). It now has been over 1 year of my credit card being charged $499/month and the software has still not been implemented. Once their sales rep made the sale and the credit card starts being charged monthly everything stopped. The customer service is absolutely horrendous. DrChrono pays someone to call you on the phone in order to implement the software for your office- unfortunately he had a heavy accent and could not be understood. Weeks and months would go by before DrChrono's customer service would schedule another time when "they were available" to teach you to implement their software. Each phone call nothing would get accomplished or implemented besides them describing another product that wanted to "up-sell" us. DrChrono doesn't care, they still get $499 a month.This same thing is sill going on now-- We have been charged $499/mo for 15 months, the software has not been implemented. Now they want to charge us thousands of additional dollars to pay for more phone calls in order to implement the software they sold my secretary over a year ago.

I wish I read the horrible reviews also posted about DrChrono stating "the biggest mistake of life", how terrible the customer service is, and there is no incentive for DrChrono to get
the software implemented since the already sold the subscription for a year

This EHR is one big money grab. This EHR is one big money grab. When a provider leaves the practice, you have to continue paying their monthly subscription until your yearly renewal date. Also, the telehealth platform is terrible and glitchy. They make patients reset their password every month or something ridiculous, and it needs to be a very complicated password, so we always have patients unable to log on to their appts on time. There are so many add ons that cost a lot of money. Customer service was OK but has gotten a lot worse recently. There are so many glitches, especially with billing that make patient's balances incorrect, and they ignore the inquiry if they don't know the answer. When pushed, they say they are working on it, but it doesn't ever get fixed. The frequency of problems has significantly increased recently. If it wasn't so difficult to switch EHRs, we would not give them our business

Outdated.. Needs a complete overhaul. It’s been the same for a decade and is seriously outdated. It used to be cutting edge and I hoped they would try to stay ahead of the curve but it’s seems as though there’s very little effort to modernize the platform. It also crashes all the time

Great program when it works. Great program when it works. A lot of customization you can do to fit any practice. However, System slowdowns and  or system going offline is a common occurrence. Billing, Training, They don't know their system. Somehow I was no longer registered for Medicare billing.  Have lost significant revenue over SEVERAL MONTHS and still not corrected.

Mediocre EHR, decent but with many issues. We use this EHR for a psychiatry practice. It meets our needs though comes with several downsides. The user interface is often times not intuitive and is cluttered. Heavy website can be slow to load in certain pages. ICD code and rx integration could be better. Poor website design leads to inefficiencies in time management which are key to running a good medical practice. There is no way to work one on one with the designers to improve website design or function. Customer service is mediocre though they try
